ledger.title: hold end/kill on a console call signals the broker, never the held process group; a SIGHUP-ignoring target escapes while hold reports success and Exited (0)
ledger.evidence: Console-mode records anchor the call to the broker: start.c:1293-1295 sets r.pid=r.pgid=r.sid=pid, and the console branch never populates target_pid_pipe (start.c:981/1017 create it only for !console_mode; broker.c:189-256 never reports its forked target's pid). broker.c installs no SIGTERM/SIGHUP handler (only SIGPIPE ignore, broker.c:293-300), so signal.c:263 kill(-r.pgid, SIGTERM) kills the broker outright: broker_cleanup_and_exit and hold_mark_run_finished never run. Verified live in an isolated store: strace showed `hold end` delivering SIGTERM to the broker pid (929932, killed with no unlink; record held pid=pgid=sid=929932 while the target was 929933); then `hold --console /bin/sh -c 'trap "" HUP; sleep 45'` + `hold end <id>` returned rc=0, printed 'stopped', list showed 'Exited (0) 3 seconds ago', the console socket stayed on disk, and dash+sleep were still running, unmanaged and invisible to hold. This violates SPEC.md:60-61 (signals delivered to the revalidated call group) and hold-on-identity.md:57 ('end the call politely: TERM, then KILL') — the held group receives neither TERM nor KILL, only an incidental PTY hangup, and the recorded exit status is silently lost for every ended console call.
ledger.remedy_hint: Have the broker write its forked target pid back through the existing handshake/target_pid_pipe path so the record stores the real group (matching the non-console and adopted paths), and give the broker a SIGTERM handler that forwards to the target group and runs broker_cleanup_and_exit.

ledger.title: hold logs -p silently truncates to the FIRST 50 records, and -n/--tail N returns the first N, not the last N, contradicting README and Docker muscle memory
ledger.evidence: src/viewer/filter.c:6 '#define VIEWER_DEFAULT_VISIBLE 50' with filter.c:29-31 defaulting both visible_capacity and max_results to it, and filter.c:139/:224 stopping the scan once line_count reaches max_results counting from the start of the file; src/runtime/signal.c:771-778 maps -n/--tail/--limit onto max_results. Probe (isolated HOME): a call logging `seq 1 5000` produced a raw log of exactly 5000 lines (verified with wc -l on <id>.log), yet `./hold logs -p <id>` printed exactly 50 lines ending at '50' (the first 50), and `./hold logs -p --tail 10 <id>` printed lines 1,2,3... This contradicts README.md:127 'hold logs -p -n 100 web  # plain text, last 100 records', docs/hold-on-identity.md:63 '--print: plain dump, always script-safe', and Docker's --tail=last-N semantics. 135/135 tests pass, so no test asserts full-dump length or last-N.
ledger.remedy_hint: Make plain (-p) dump the whole log by default, make -n/--tail select the last N records (sidecar makes this cheap), and pin both shapes with tests in test_hold.sh.

ledger.title: --restart calls never record their final exit status; ended restart calls render a false 'Exited (0)' and inspect stays 'running' forever
ledger.evidence: run_restart_supervisor (start.c:288-372) ends with _exit(0) and never calls hold_mark_run_finished; the waiter process that does mark finished (start.c:1106-1117) is skipped whenever restart is enabled (start.c:1017 creates target_pid_pipe only when '!console_mode && !restart_enabled'; start.c:1060-1084 diverts to the supervisor). Probe on the built binary: 'hold -d --restart on-failure:1 --restart-delay 0 /bin/sh -c "exit 3"' -> after both runs failed with status 3, 'hold list' shows 'Exited (0) 1 second ago' and 'hold inspect' shows "Status": "running", "ExitCode": 0, "state": "running". docker-parity-contract.md:56-58 pins Docker STATUS phrasing where 'Exited (0)' means clean exit; the table now carries fabricated success for failed services.
ledger.remedy_hint: Have the supervisor call hold_mark_run_finished with the last worker status before _exit (it already knows status), or reintroduce a waiter around the supervisor; add a shell test asserting Exited (N) after a restart policy gives up.

ledger.title: -e/--env and --env-file setenv() into hold's own process before store/invocation resolution, so 'hold -e HOME=X cmd' silently relocates the call record store
ledger.evidence: append_env_assignment calls setenv(key, eq+1, 1) in the hold process (cli_main.c:266), and --env-file routes every file line through it (cli_main.c:317). Flag parsing runs (cli_main.c:520-527) before hold_detect_invocation (693) and hold_ensure_user_store_for_current_user (745), which reads getenv("HOME") (store/layout.c:49). Probe: './hold -e HOME=$FAKE -d /bin/sleep 0.3' wrote its record under $FAKE/.local/state/hold (1 record) and none under the real store. docker-parity-contract.md:16-27 says -e/--env-file behave exactly like docker run counterparts, i.e. configure only the launch environment; here they reconfigure hold itself (store location, PATH-based binary resolution at start.c:830, and the HOLD_TEST_* hooks).
ledger.remedy_hint: Stop mutating hold's own environment; the child already applies the recorded env array via apply_child_env (start.c:1134). Keep -e purely as recipe data.

ledger.title: Foreground bare launch always exits 0 regardless of the command's exit status, unlike docker run
ledger.evidence: hold_tail_log_until_exit returns 0 unconditionally on normal completion (signal.c:173-260), and the foreground path uses that as the CLI exit code (start.c:1503, returned at 1517). Probe: './hold /bin/sh -c "exit 7"' -> rc 0; './hold /bin/false' -> rc 0. docker run in the foreground propagates the container's exit code, and the bare launch form is the surface docker-parity-contract.md:13-30 governs; no test in tests/test_hold.sh asserts foreground exit-code behavior (foreground tests at 3423-3450 check only output shape), so the gap is unpinned. Scripts using 'hold cmd && next' silently proceed past failures. Basis for parity claim: Docker's documented/observed run semantics (judgment); the rc-0 behavior itself is tool-verified fact.
ledger.remedy_hint: Foreground path should read the marked-finished record after tail ends and exit with the recorded code (waiter already writes it); pin with a shell test, or document the deliberate divergence in the parity contract.

ledger.title: Leader-gone fallback signals a group on bare (pgid,sid) match; recorded identity token is checked for existence, never revalidated — violates SPEC safety invariant as written
ledger.evidence: signal.c:150-157: when the leader's /proc stat is unreadable (leader reaped), validate_signal_target falls to `gl = hold_group_session_liveness(r->pgid, r->sid); if (gl == GROUP_LIVE) { if (!have_identity_token) {...return 2;} return 0; }` — have_identity_token (line 112) only tests that r->proc_starttime_ticks or exe_dev/ino are nonzero in the record; nothing compares them to any live process. hold_do_signal_action then runs kill(-r.pgid, sig) at signal.c:263, and the post-TERM revalidation at signal.c:286 goes through the same fallback before the SIGKILL at 298. SPEC.md:59-60 pins: 'Signals are delivered to the group only after the recorded identity (start time, exe identity) revalidates — a recycled PID is never signaled.' Hold-launched calls have pid==pgid==sid (setsid), so one recycled PID that daemonizes reproduces the pair.
ledger.remedy_hint: Either harden the fallback (e.g. require each surviving member's starttime >= recorded leader starttime and record/compare the session-leader token, or refuse leaderless groups without an explicit override) or narrow SPEC.md:59-60 to what the code guarantees; add a shell test for the leader-gone path (test_hold.sh:750 covers only the live-leader tamper case).
ledger.failure_scenario: Call's group dies while its record still reads running (supervisor killed, or stale adopted record); PID counter wraps; an unrelated same-user process gets pid P == recorded sid, calls setsid() (pgid=sid=P), forks a worker, then exits. `hold stop <call>`: leader stat for P fails -> GROUP_LIVE fallback matches the foreign worker -> kill(-P, SIGTERM) and, after 5s, SIGKILL hit an unrelated process.

ledger.title: Crash-leftover .<id>.tmp permanently blocks all future record rewrites for that call; no sweep or recovery path exists
ledger.evidence: hold_write_record_atomic opens a fixed-name temp with O_EXCL (record.c:163) and unlinks it only on its own failure (record.c:384-386). A tmp left by a crash/SIGKILL mid-write is never cleaned: purge's sweep matches only *.json/*.log/*.sock/public (list.c:662-827). Probed in an isolated store: with .<id>.tmp planted, 'hold rename' died fatally 'failed to write renamed call record: File exists' (hold_die_errno at call.c:267); after killing the held process the record still read "state": "running" with no exit_code/ended_at because the reaper's 50x100ms retry loop (start.c:1111-1117) exhausted silently; 'hold purge -a' removed the call but left the .tmp behind.
ledger.remedy_hint: On EEXIST, unlink a stale same-name tmp (it can only be a dead writer's leftover since the id embeds creation material) or use hold_open_unique_temp (fs.c:60) + rename; additionally have the purge sweep collect .*.tmp/.*.reserve litter.

ledger.title: Purge sweep deletes a concurrently-starting call's log and console socket because they exist before the record JSON
ledger.evidence: Start creates the log at start.c:924-932 (open_log_append_no_symlink, O_CREAT) and the console socket during spawn, but writes <id>.json only at start.c:1408 — after name generation, fork/exec and console setup. cmd_prune_store_all's orphan sweeps unlink any <id>.log (list.c:748-752) or <id>.sock (list.c:784-786) whose runs/<id>.json is absent, and never consult the .<id>.reserve marker that start holds for exactly this window (start.c:915, list.c sweep has no reserve check). A concurrent 'hold purge' during a start therefore unlinks the nascent log (capture continues into an unlinked inode; 'hold logs'/viewer then fail) and the live console socket.
ledger.remedy_hint: Treat presence of record_dir/.<id>.reserve as 'call being created' in the log/console/public orphan sweeps (mirror id_collides_in_store which already checks it, layout.c:169).

ledger.title: Broker treats EAGAIN on the nonblocking attach client as fatal, silently dropping a live attach during an output burst
ledger.evidence: broker.c:362 sets the accepted client nonblocking, then broker.c:410 (live stream) and broker.c:370 (initial 64KB replay) use hold_write_all, which handles only EINTR — any EAGAIN returns -1 (src/core/util.c:41-59) and the broker closes the client (broker.c:410-414). Verified live: attached to a console call, stalled reading for 14s through a 600KB burst; the broker closed the connection (EOF after 180188 bytes) while `hold list` still showed the call Up. The real `hold attach` client renders this as read()==0 -> break with rc=0 (attach.c:237-238): the session vanishes silently, indistinguishable from the call exiting. Any client slower than the producer for ~208KB (Linux default AF_UNIX buffering; far less where wmem is tuned down or on BSD/macOS ~8KB defaults, where even the 64KB replay write at broker.c:370 can fail the attach outright) is disconnected mid-stream.
ledger.remedy_hint: On EAGAIN, poll the client for POLLOUT (bounded) or buffer bounded per-client output and drop the client only past a threshold; at minimum distinguish EAGAIN from hard errors in the client write path, and let the attach client report an abnormal drop instead of exiting 0.

ledger.title: After PTY EOF with the target still running, the broker blocks in waitpid with the listener open — attach connects and hangs forever with no response
ledger.evidence: broker_serve breaks its loop on master EOF/EIO (broker.c:415-417) and then does a blocking waitpid (broker.c:421-426) while the listener and socket remain alive; accept() is never called again. Verified live: `hold --console /bin/sh -c 'exec >/dev/null 2>&1 </dev/null; sleep 300'` left the broker in wchan=do_wait with its listener socket open; a client connect + magic then hung >5s with no data, no denial, no EOF, while list showed the call Up. A real `hold attach` user sits in a raw-mode alternate screen showing nothing (only Ctrl-P Ctrl-Q escapes, since ISIG is cleared by hold_make_raw_termios, frame.c:204). Targets that daemonize their stdio are exactly the processes hold exists to hold.
ledger.remedy_hint: Keep serving (or at least accepting and refusing with a message, e.g. 'console closed, call still running — see hold logs') while waiting for the child: waitpid(WNOHANG) in a poll loop on the listener instead of the post-loop blocking wait.

ledger.title: Legacy JSON-log shim silently rewrites user output in every read path, including the script-safe plain dump
ledger.evidence: Probe (isolated HOME, built ./hold): a call printing the literal line {"log":"SNEAKY-decoded","stream":"stdout"} has that exact text in the raw .log, but `hold logs <id> -p` prints only `SNEAKY-decoded`. consume_line (src/viewer/filter.c:204), the backward scanner (filter.c:437), and the tail path (src/runtime/signal.c:50) all pass every line through hold_decode_json_log_line (src/core/logging.c:389-412), which replaces any line starting with '{' that contains a "log" key. No writer produces this JSON format anymore (hold_write_json_log_* forward to the raw writer, logging.c:381-387), so the decode is a pure legacy shim that corrupts faithful display, and filtering/exclusion match against the transformed text, not what is on disk. Contradicts SPEC.md:50-54 (plain output = raw captured bytes) and identity.md:63 (-p 'always script-safe').
ledger.remedy_hint: Delete hold_decode_json_log_line and its three call sites (plus the dead hold_write_json_log_* wrappers); emit raw line bytes. Matches the no-legacy-shims workstyle; add a regression test asserting a {"log":...} user line round-trips verbatim through logs -p.

ledger.title: -n/--tail N prints the FIRST N lines, not the newest N, inverting the Docker-borrowed tail semantics
ledger.evidence: Probe: for a 6-line log, `hold logs <id> -n 2 -p` printed `one` and `two` (oldest two). The parser aliases -n/--tail/--limit to opts.max_results (signal.c:771-782); the non-follow plain path runs hold_log_filter_fd forward from offset 0 and stops after max_results matches (signal.c:892-899, filter.c:139/224) instead of anchoring backward from EOF via hold_log_filter_backward_fd, which already exists and does exactly newest-N (filter.c:333). Identity.md:60 pins `hold logs <call> [-f] [-n N]`; the --tail spelling is Docker's, where it unambiguously means the most recent N. Additionally -n is silently ignored in the interactive viewer: configure_filter_opts overwrites visible_capacity/max_results with viewer_body_rows (src/viewer/tty.c:551-553). No test in tests/test_hold.sh or test_040_dockerish.sh asserts -n/--tail behavior.
ledger.remedy_hint: Route the plain non-follow path with a limit through hold_log_filter_backward_fd anchored at EOF (uncapped byte budget), and pin the newest-N shape in test_hold.sh.

ledger.title: Sidecar staleness fields are written on every append but never validated, so a truncated/rotated raw log yields silently wrong timestamps and stream labels
ledger.evidence: append_logidx_entry persists raw_size_bytes/raw_mtime_ns into the header on every record (logging.c:340-341), but hold_logidx_map_load (logging.c:242-303) and hold_open_log_index_fd (logging.c:169-207) never compare them against the actual raw log. Compiled probe: after truncating the raw log and appending one new stderr line, reopen was accepted, the map held non-ascending offsets [0,4,0] — violating the 'ascending by offset' invariant the binary search depends on (include/hold/core.h:106, logging.c:305-318) — and hold_logidx_map_find(0) returned the OLD stdout record (meta 0x0) for the NEW stderr line: the viewer would render a fabricated timestamp and OUT label as truth, defeating the stated 'never fake metadata' rule (src/viewer/filter.c:115). Reachable in practice via logrotate copytruncate on a long-held call's log.
ledger.remedy_hint: On load/reopen, if header raw_size_bytes > current raw size (or mtime regressed), treat the sidecar as stale: rebuild/truncate it and render without metadata. One check in hold_logidx_map_load and one in hold_open_log_index_fd; the fields already exist for exactly this.

ledger.title: The 'documented, stable' HLOGIDX format has no current-format doc, and the only written byte layout contradicts the shipped meta bits
ledger.evidence: SPEC.md:50-53 and hold-on-identity.md:29-31 promise the .log/.log.idx pair 'stays a documented, stable format so anything else can read Hold's logs without Hold's help'. No normative doc (SPEC.md, store.md, docker-parity-contract.md) documents the byte layout; docs/store.md never mentions the sidecar at all. The only byte-level spec is the proposal in docs/future/viewer-fixes-before-0.5.md, which defines a 3-bit stream field (STREAM_MASK 0x0007, STDOUT=0/STDERR=1/STDIN=2/PTY=3) with NO_NEWLINE=0x0008/CONTINUATION=0x0010/TRUNCATED=0x0020, while the shipped writer uses single-bit flags STDERR=0x0001, NO_NEWLINE=0x0002, CONTINUATION=0x0004, TRUNCATED=0x0008 (logging.c:13-16). Probe confirmed on disk: a partial stdout line writes meta=0x0002, which a reader following the written doc decodes as stream STDIN, and every stderr partial line as STDIN|corrupted flags.
ledger.remedy_hint: Write the actual v1 layout (header offsets, entry packing, meta bits, crash-recovery min-count rule) into docs/store.md or SPEC.md as the normative format, and mark the future-doc layout superseded. Pin it with a byte-level test.

ledger.title: Capability machinery (--cap-add/--cap-drop) is fully alive and test-pinned but ordered deleted by the identity doc and documented nowhere user-facing
ledger.evidence: Cut-table answer, verified end to end this session. Parse: src/cli_main.c:397-428 accepts --cap-add/--cap-drop (both split and = forms) on the bare launch form. Apply: src/runtime/start.c:627-671 implements apply_child_capabilities via raw SYS_capget/SYS_capset plus PR_CAPBSET_DROP; called on launch (start.c:1139) and re-applied by the restart worker (start.c:347-348). Persist: recipes round-trip through the record store with Docker-style CapAdd/CapDrop JSON keys (src/store/record.c:136-140, 292-300, 762-774) and project into inspect (verified live: inspect shows "CapDrop": ["ALL"]). Tests pin all three behaviors (tests/test_hold.sh:2517, 2539, 2562, registered 4213-4215). Runtime probe (isolated HOME): non-root `hold --rm --cap-drop ALL -- sh -c 'grep CapEff /proc/self/status'` yields CapEff 0000000000000000 (works silently for any user, not just root); non-root --cap-add fails honestly but opaquely: "hold: cannot start '/usr/bin/true': Operation not permitted" (never names capabilities). Yet docs/hold-on-identity.md:187 marks "grants, sudoers pinning, elevation, --cap paths (src/access/)" as "delete — cruft"; docker-parity-contract.md:16-27's exhaustive borrowed-flag list omits both flags; `./hold --help | grep -ci cap` = 0; no help topic in src/cli.c mentions them. The rest of that cut row IS executed: src/access/invocation.c (read in full, 62 lines) contains only sudo-provenance/store-context detection — no grants, sudoers, or elevation — and docs/index.md:49 already re-scopes access/ as "invocation context". So tests assert behavior the superior contract doc orders deleted and the subordinate contract does not license.
ledger.remedy_hint: Redline the decision one way: either execute the cut (delete the flags, apply_child_capabilities, recipe fields, and the three tests) or keep it and add --cap-add/--cap-drop to the docker-parity-contract flag list and help; if kept, make the non-root --cap-add EPERM message name the capability request.

ledger.title: Top-level --help claims "ps is an alias" of list, contradicting the normative contract, the code's own dispatch, and the pinned test behavior
ledger.evidence: hold_usage() prints "hold list — the call table (ps is an alias)" (src/runtime/commands.c:79; confirmed in live `./hold` output). docker-parity-contract.md:38-39 states "ps is no longer an alias of list"; hold-on-identity.md:95-136 dedicates a section to their divergence (different columns, list-only scope flags). The code agrees with the docs, not the help: cli_main.c:713-714 comments "ps is deliberately NOT folded onto list", list/ps dispatch separately (cli_main.c:814-847), and tests pin the divergence — `hold ps -l/-s/-u` are rejected as unknown flags rc=1 (tests/test_hold.sh:2807-2812). Every user running bare `hold` (the pinned Docker-parity help path, test_hold.sh:815-819 asserts 'PLACE A CALL' on stdout rc=0) is told the two divergent verbs are interchangeable. No test pins the alias sentence, so the fix is one string.
ledger.remedy_hint: Replace the parenthetical with the real relationship, e.g. "hold list — your scoped ledger; hold ps — Docker's machine-wide running view".

ledger.title: Undocumented --privileged flag silently remaps Docker's capability-granting flag to system-store scoping — leftover elevation-era vocabulary
ledger.evidence: parse_docker_run_flag accepts --privileged (src/cli_main.c:136-139) and parse_run_options remaps it to requested_system (cli_main.c:342-343), i.e. "place this call in the root-managed store" — not Docker's "grant all capabilities". Verified live (isolated HOME, non-root): `./hold --privileged /bin/true` exits 3 with "hold: error: the root-managed store requires root; re-run as root or with sudo" — a message that never mentions the flag the user typed. The flag appears in no current doc: docker-parity-contract.md:16-27's borrowed-flag list omits it (and its honesty clause at lines 33-35 rejects substrate flags -p/-P/-v "never faked"); hold-on-identity.md's surface never mentions it; the only mentions are in docs/archive/ (UX_REVIEW.md:194 lists --privileged as the deleted "explicit high-risk privileged/elevated mode"). grep of tests/test_hold.sh finds zero coverage. A Docker user's --privileged muscle memory is silently reinterpreted, which is exactly the misleading-borrowed-flag behavior the parity contract exists to prevent.
ledger.remedy_hint: Delete the --privileged branch (it duplicates the leading --system switch); if any spelling is kept, reject it with an honest message pointing at sudo/--system, and pin that in a test.

ledger.title: Flags-only invocation (e.g. `hold -d` with no command) prints the full human help to stdout while exiting 5, violating the stdout-machine-only contract
ledger.evidence: cli_main.c:549-552: after the leading-flag loop, `if (argi >= argc) { hold_usage(); return 5; }` — hold_usage() writes to stdout (src/runtime/commands.c:66 printf). Verified live: `./hold --quiet` exits 5 with 1707 bytes on stdout and 0 bytes on stderr. docker-parity-contract.md:66-69 (normative, tests assert its shapes): "stdout carries machine data only: IDs, tables, logs, JSON. Human notes, hints, and errors go to stderr". Every other usage-error path honors this (fprintf(stderr,...) at cli.c:206-207, cli_main.c:350, 359, etc.). Failure scenario: a script does `id=$(hold -d "$CMD")` with $CMD accidentally empty — instead of an empty id and a stderr diagnostic, $id captures the entire help screen. The deliberate bare-`hold` help-to-stdout path (cli_main.c:499-502, rc 0, pinned by tests/test_hold.sh:815-819) is distinct and unaffected.
ledger.remedy_hint: In the argi >= argc failure path, print a one-line usage error to stderr (keep exit 5, or 1 per the exit-code table) instead of hold_usage() to stdout.

ledger.title: Every launch silently rewrites any argv token that happens to name an existing file into an absolute path, changing the command's semantics
ledger.evidence: hold_normalize_existing_argv_paths_from_cwd (paths.c:147-201) realpath-rewrites every argv[i] (and every '--flag=value' value) that resolves to an existing path. start.c:859 applies it to launch_argv on EVERY launch and start.c:1191 execs that rewritten argv. Probe (isolated HOME, cwd containing file 'hello' and dir 'build'): `hold echo hello build nonexistent` printed '/tmp/tmp.fozKi7qvi5/hello /tmp/tmp.fozKi7qvi5/build nonexistent' — the child received rewritten arguments, not what the user typed. Neither docker-parity-contract.md nor SPEC.md sanctions this; docs/store.md:70 pins only argv[0] resolution ('The recorded argv[0] is the resolved executable path'). Non-path arguments that collide with common names (make targets 'test'/'build', subcommand words) are silently converted — plain `echo hello` and `hold echo hello` disagree.
ledger.remedy_hint: Constrain rewriting to the recorded recipe (redial portability) or to tokens the user marked as paths; the exec'd argv should be byte-identical to what was typed except argv[0]. At minimum pin the current behavior in a normative doc + test if it is intended.

ledger.title: hold_valid_alias accepts flag-look-alike call names ('-d', '--force'), diverging from Docker's --name grammar the parity contract binds to
ledger.evidence: validate.c:73-78 accepts '-' and '_' in any position including the first (only isalnum|'_'|'-' per char, no first-char rule). Probe: `hold -d --name -d sleep 5` succeeded and `hold list` rendered NAMES cell '-d'. docker-parity-contract.md:13-23 says these flags 'behave exactly like their docker run counterparts'; Docker's name grammar requires a leading alphanumeric ([a-zA-Z0-9][a-zA-Z0-9_.-]*) and rejects '-d' outright. A name colliding with hold's own flag syntax is a usability trap (targetable only where the parser happens to treat it as a positional token) and is silently accepted where Docker errors. hold is also stricter than Docker in rejecting '.', undocumented.
ledger.remedy_hint: Require a leading alphanumeric in hold_valid_alias (and decide on '.'), matching the Docker grammar the contract borrows; add a shell test asserting rejection of '-d'-style names.

ledger.title: --privileged is silently accepted and remapped to "system store", contradicting Docker muscle memory, the parity contract's closed flag list, and the identity cut of elevation cruft
ledger.evidence: cli_main.c:136-137 accepts --privileged in parse_docker_run_flag; cli_main.c:342-343 maps it to *requested_system = true. For a non-root user this hits the refusal at cli_main.c:730-736 (exit 3, "the root-managed store requires root"); for root it is a no-op because start.c:1553 already sets wants_system_store from euid_root. docker-parity-contract.md:16-27 enumerates the borrowed flags (-d,-i,-t,-e,--env-file,--name,--rm,--restart,--detach-keys) and --privileged is absent; grep of docs/ and src/cli.c help text shows zero documentation, and grep of tests/ shows zero test pins it. Docker's --privileged grants capabilities; hold reinterprets it as store scoping — misleading borrowed behavior.
ledger.remedy_hint: Delete the --privileged branch (cli_main.c:136-138) and the privileged field/mapping (~12 lines). No test or doc depends on it — verified by grep of tests/ and docs/.

ledger.title: hold --help and README still say 'ps is an alias' of list, contradicting the pinned 0.6.0 divergence and actual behavior
ledger.evidence: src/runtime/commands.c:79 emits 'hold list  the call table (ps is an alias)' (observed in ./hold --help), and README.md:94 repeats it. But docker-parity-contract.md:38-39 says 'ps is no longer an alias of list', cli_main.c:712-714 comments 'ps is deliberately NOT folded onto list: they now diverge', CHANGELOG 0.6.0 head says 'ps speaks only Docker; list speaks only Hold', and the binary confirms divergence: usage 'hold list [-a|-s|-u|-l] [name]' vs 'hold ps [-a] [name]', with `hold help list`/`hold help ps` describing different column sets (USER column only in list).
ledger.remedy_hint: Reword the --help line and README.md:94 to present list (scoped ledger) and ps (Docker mirror) as distinct verbs, matching hold help list/ps.

ledger.title: Launch surface accepts undocumented flags the identity cut table marked delete: --cap-add/--cap-drop perform real Linux capset changes; --restart-delay is also doc-absent
ledger.evidence: cli_main.c:397-425 parses --cap-add/--cap-drop; start.c:627-671 apply_child_capabilities does SYS_capget/SYS_capset plus PR_CAPBSET_DROP on the child; call.c:149-152 carries them into the redial recipe. Probe: `./hold --cap-drop ALL -d sh -c 'sleep 0.2'` was accepted and printed a 64-hex id. hold-on-identity.md:188 cut table row says '--cap paths (src/access/) | delete — cruft; do one thing well', and the exclusive borrowed-flag lists (docker-parity-contract.md:17-27, hold-on-identity.md:151-153, README.md:70-72, hold --help) mention none of them; only docs/archive/* documents them. cli_main.c:461 also parses --restart-delay, documented only in archive (though tested at tests/test_hold.sh:2573); --cap-* has no shell test at all.
ledger.remedy_hint: Either execute the cut (delete --cap-add/--cap-drop parsing and apply_child_capabilities) or redline the identity/parity contracts to sanction them with tests; document or fold --restart-delay into the --restart contract.

ledger.title: index.md's current 'Technical references' (launcher.md, store.md, console.md) document the deleted pre-0.6 world — profiles, hold start/console/tail/dump, 12-hex IDs, self-elevation — with broken links
ledger.evidence: docs/index.md:29-32 lists them as current references and SPEC.md:7-8 cites them as mechanism references, yet: launcher.md:9 claims 'a bare 12-hex run ID on stdout' (docker-parity-contract.md:17-18 pins full 64-hex; CHANGELOG 0.6.1: 'the ancient 12-hex full-id format are gone'; probe printed 64-hex); launcher.md:16-18 documents 'hold start <profile>'; store.md:7/21/37-43 document profiles.json, aliases.json, 'hold tail <id>', profile maps; console.md:7 documents 'hold --console' and 'hold console <target>' plus 'Profiles and console mode' (:50-52) and root self-elevation (:96) which cli_main.c:727-729 says is gone. Probes confirm `hold dump|profile|start` fall through to PATH launch ('hold: cannot start 'dump': command not found'). Their nav bars link quickstart.md, profiles-and-aliases.md, security.md, cli-contract.md, target-resolution.md — all now only under docs/archive/, so the relative links are dead.
ledger.remedy_hint: Rewrite launcher/store/console (and touch identity.md's 'run' vocabulary) against the 0.6 surface, or move them to archive/ and let SPEC.md + store.md-successor carry the invariants; fix or drop the dead nav links.

ledger.title: The only shipped example script is built on the deleted profile system and cannot run against the current binary
ledger.evidence: examples/uv-webserver-profile.sh:143 runs '"$HOLD_BIN" profile save "$RUN_ID" as "$PROFILE"', :151 'hold start "$PROFILE"', :84/:168 'hold dump', :86/:147 'hold prune <id>' — 'profile', 'start', and 'dump' are not in the verb table (src/cli.c:25-47) and probes show they fall through to PATH launch ('hold: cannot start 'profile': command not found'). examples/README.md:7 still advertises 'creates a durable profile from the recorded run... then starts the profile'. hold-on-identity.md:157-159: 'There is no profile system.'
ledger.remedy_hint: Rewrite the example around save/rename/redial (the stated profile replacement) and rename the file; update examples/README.md accordingly.

ledger.title: HOLD_TEST_FAIL_RECORD_WRITE / HOLD_TEST_FAIL_PUBLIC_INDEX_WRITE getenv hooks are compiled into production builds, unlike every other test hook
ledger.evidence: start.c:1406 and 1441 read these env vars with no #ifdef HOLD_TESTING guard, while all sibling hooks are guarded (store/layout.c:111-112, runtime/list.c:436-437, access/invocation.c:30-31) and Makefile:30 pins that test-only behavior 'can never be linked' into the product. Probe on the production ./hold: 'HOLD_TEST_FAIL_RECORD_WRITE=1 ./hold -d /bin/sleep 5' -> rc 1, 'hold: failed to write record: Input/output error', process spawned then rolled back. Any environment leaking this variable (or 'hold -e HOLD_TEST_FAIL_RECORD_WRITE=1 ...' via the finding above) makes every launch spawn-then-kill.
ledger.remedy_hint: Wrap both getenv fault-injection sites in #ifdef HOLD_TESTING to match the repo's own guard discipline.

ledger.title: Record loader's Docker State.ExitCode fallback turns every unmarked death into a false clean 'Exited (0)', making the honest 'Exited (?)' path unreachable
ledger.evidence: hold_write_record_atomic always emits State.ExitCode, writing 0 when no exit code is known (record.c:97: '(r && r->has_exit_code) ? r->exit_code : 0'). The loader falls back to that field (record.c:661-665), setting has_exit_code=true with 0 for every running-state record. So list.c's honest branch 'Exited (?)' (list.c:135) can never trigger for records hold itself wrote: any call that dies without hold_mark_run_finished (restart supervisor path, waiter killed, power loss) displays 'Exited (0)' — the parity-contract STATUS phrase for a clean exit (docker-parity-contract.md:56-58).
ledger.remedy_hint: Only trust State.ExitCode when the record's state is exited/finished; otherwise leave has_exit_code false so the 'Exited (?)' honesty path works.

ledger.title: Redial and action verbs use contradictory id-vs-name resolution ladders; the same token errors 'ambiguous' on redial but silently resolves for logs/end
ledger.evidence: find_restart_record (call.c:40-80) merges id-prefix hits and name hits into one match count, so a token matching one call's id prefix and another call's name yields matches=2 -> 'ambiguous' (rc 6). hold_resolve_action_token instead tries the name tier first and returns on first hit (resolve.c:258-292). Probe: with call1 id bd4a... and call2 --name bd4a, './hold bd4a' -> rc 6 "call 'bd4a' is ambiguous" while './hold logs bd4a -p' -> rc 0 (silently the named call). Both contradict the documented order — cli_main.c:804-805 comment: 'call id -> call name -> PATH command' — under which the id match should win deterministically.
ledger.remedy_hint: Make find_restart_record tiered (exact id, then unique id prefix, then name) and align hold_resolve_action_token's tier order with it; pin with a collision test.

ledger.title: --rm combines with --restart without complaint; docker run rejects the combination
ledger.evidence: The --rm branch (cli_main.c:479-483) and restart parsing (445-478) have no mutual-exclusion check, and start.c validates only restart+tty (start.c:823-825). Probe: './hold --rm --restart on-failure:1 -d /bin/sh -c "exit 0"' -> rc 0, id printed. docker run fails this with 'Conflicting options: --restart and --rm'; docker-parity-contract.md:16-27 pins --rm and --restart to behave exactly like their docker run counterparts. Combined with the unrecorded restart exit status (finding 1), the --rm watcher then prunes a call whose failure history is unrecoverable.
ledger.remedy_hint: Reject --rm together with an active --restart policy at parse time with docker's phrasing, and pin with a test next to test_docker_restart_validation_and_tty_gate.

ledger.title: hold_usage() claims 'ps is an alias' of list, contradicting the parity contract and the code's own deliberately divergent ps/list
ledger.evidence: commands.c:79 prints '  hold list                    the call table (ps is an alias)'; probed in the binary ('./hold | grep alias' shows the line). docker-parity-contract.md:38-39 states 'they are distinct verbs (ps is no longer an alias of list)', cli_main.c:713-714 comments 'ps is deliberately NOT folded onto list', and behavior diverges (ps: machine-wide, only -a, no USER column — cli.c help_action text agrees). The primary help surface teaches users the pre-0.6 model.
ledger.remedy_hint: Reword the usage line, e.g. 'hold list — your scoped ledger' plus a separate 'hold ps — Docker's machine-wide view' entry.

ledger.title: Explicit --system / sudo store selection is silently overridden into the user store when any argv path resolves under the invoking home
ledger.evidence: hold_ensure_start_store_for_command (start.c:1546-1566) redirects wants_system_store (which includes an explicit requested_system flag) to the user store whenever hold_start_target_is_within_invoking_home returns true; that helper (start.c:721-772) matches not just the target binary but any argument — including the value after '=' in any '-flag=/path' arg (753-770) — that resolves under $HOME. So 'sudo hold --system -d tool --config=/home/rich/x.conf' lands in rich's user store with no notice, contradicting the tool's own help ('Root and sudo calls use the root-managed store', cli.c help_system) and hold-on-identity.md's silence on any such heuristic. Neither normative doc mentions this override.
ledger.remedy_hint: At minimum never override an explicit --system request (honor the flag or refuse loudly), narrow the heuristic to the resolved executable, and print a stderr note when redirecting; document whatever is kept.

ledger.title: start.c remains oversized/mixed-responsibility: an undocumented ~240-line capability engine, a repurposed --privileged flag, and dead output paths survive the 0.6.1 split
ledger.evidence: start.c is 1,567 lines. Lines 440-678 are a Linux capability name table plus apply_child_capabilities for --cap-add/--cap-drop; cli_main.c:136-139 and 343-344 map docker's --privileged to 'use the root-managed store' — semantics unrelated to docker's. None of --cap-add/--cap-drop/--privileged appear in docker-parity-contract.md's borrowed-flag list (16-27) or hold-on-identity.md's parity enumeration (151-154); grep of docs/ finds no mention (only archive-era console.md). Dead code: hold_perform_start_options' only callers both run with inv.docker_run=true (cli_main.c:701/809, call.c:161), so the non-docker_run banner branch (start.c:1478-1494, still advertising 'hold tail'/'hold stop') is unreachable, as is explicit_start_argv0's command=="start" compare (start.c:381) since 'start' is absent from cli.c's command_specs (25-48); include/hold/types.h:109 still documents docker_run as 'invoked via the Docker-shaped run verb', a deleted verb. Basis: owner workstyle (no ballooning, contracts pinned in docs+tests) and the identity doc's own cut discipline.
ledger.remedy_hint: Either add --cap-add/--cap-drop (and --privileged's real meaning) to the parity contract or cut them; delete the dead non-docker_run branch and the stale 'start' compare; consider extracting the capability engine and restart supervisor into their own compilation units.

ledger.title: Usage/help text is printed to stdout on error paths (exit 5/1), violating the pinned output discipline
ledger.evidence: cli_main.c:549-551 ('hold -d' with no command) calls hold_usage() — which printf's to stdout (commands.c:65-96) — then returns 5; hold_perform_start_options does the same on invalid options (start.c:778-781, 819-821), and the unknown-command fallthrough prints usage to stdout with rc 1 (cli_main.c:930-932). docker-parity-contract.md:66-69: 'stdout carries machine data only ... errors go to stderr'. Failure scenario: id=$(hold -d) captures the entire usage banner into a variable a script then treats as an id.
ledger.remedy_hint: Route usage output to stderr whenever the exit code is nonzero (keep stdout for the deliberate bare 'hold' help, rc 0).

ledger.title: exe-identity revalidation fails open: an unreadable /proc/<pid>/exe is treated as a pass, then the mere existence of the token authorizes the kill
ledger.evidence: signal.c:121-129: the exe check runs only when r->proc_starttime_ticks == 0, and rejects only `if (hold_read_proc_exe(r->pid, ...) == 0 && (exe_dev != r->exe_dev || exe_ino != r->exe_ino))` — a -1 return (EACCES on another uid's recycled pid, ENOENT race) silently passes; lines 137-142 then return 0 because have_identity_token is true (exe fields are nonzero in the record). Verification failure is converted into verification success. Same fail-open pattern in hold_eval_state at src/runtime/state.c:54-59, where an unreadable exe suppresses STALE detection.
ledger.remedy_hint: Treat hold_read_proc_exe failure as identity-not-confirmed when it is the only token (refuse with the existing 'no recorded process identity token' message or a distinct 'identity unverifiable' one), mirroring the strict starttime handling at signal.c:117-119.
ledger.failure_scenario: Legacy/adopted record with proc_starttime_ticks=0 and exe token set; recorded pid recycled by a process whose exe link cannot be stat'd. Validation passes on a token that was never compared; kill(-pgid) is attempted — cross-uid it degrades to a misleading EPERM exit 3 instead of the honest 'identity differs' exit 2; same-uid it signals the wrong group.

ledger.title: Irreducible TOCTOU window between revalidation and kill(-pgid) makes SPEC's absolute 'a recycled PID is never signaled' an overclaim
ledger.evidence: signal.c:250 (validate_signal_target reads /proc) -> signal.c:263 `kill(-r.pgid, sig)`, and signal.c:286 (revalidate) -> signal.c:298 `kill(-r.pgid, SIGKILL)`: between the /proc reads and the syscall the whole group can exit and the (pgid,sid) pair be recycled. Linux offers pidfd_send_signal only for single processes, not process groups, so no atomic identity-checked group kill exists; the code does the best available sequencing (revalidate immediately before each kill) but the window cannot be closed. SPEC.md:60 states the guarantee absolutely.
ledger.remedy_hint: Doc-side fix is cheapest and matches the owner's pin-contracts style: reword SPEC.md:59-60 to 'never knowingly signaled; identity is revalidated immediately before every delivery'. Optionally shrink the window by re-reading the leader stat token as the last act before kill.
ledger.failure_scenario: hold stop validates the target, the entire group exits, and (with a small pid_max and heavy fork churn) the pgid is recycled before the kill syscall lands; the TERM or the 1s-later KILL hits an unrelated group despite revalidation having passed microseconds earlier.

ledger.title: hold_proc_read_ids is a near-verbatim duplicate of platform read_process_ids_state, but dropped the pid-token validation — two diverging /proc stat parsers
ledger.evidence: observe.c:45-97 duplicates src/platform/process.c:42-108 (same open/read/strrchr-')' /strtok_r structure), except the observe copy parses with bare `pgid = (pid_t)strtol(tok, NULL, 10);` (observe.c:81,84) where the platform copy routes through parse_pid_token (process.c:29-38: end/errno/positivity checks, failing the parse on malformed tokens). A third stat parser lives in hold_read_proc_stat_tokens (process.c:251-320) and a fourth partial one in hold_proc_read_cpu_rss (observe.c:99-135). All four feed the same group-membership/identity decisions.
ledger.remedy_hint: Consolidate onto the platform parser (export read_process_ids_state or extend hold_read_proc_stat_tokens with pgid/sid outputs) and delete hold_proc_read_ids — a pure subtraction consistent with the no-ballooning workstyle.
ledger.failure_scenario: A future fix to the stat parser (field index, comm edge case, validation) lands in one copy and not the other; observe-side group matching (stats/ports membership at observe.c:216-244) silently diverges from the platform liveness scan used by the signal path.

ledger.title: Purge sweep removes the .log but orphans the .log.idx sidecar forever
ledger.evidence: The sweep path unlinks r.log_path only (list.c:700-703), unlike targeted hold_prune_one_run which also calls unlink_log_index_for_log (list.c:640-643). The later orphan-log pass matches only names ending '.log' (list.c:724), so '<id>.log.idx' is never collected. Probed: after 'hold -d sh -c exit 0' then 'hold purge', the store retained 249365...ccd2.log.idx; a second purge reported 'nothing to purge' and left it. A second orphaned idx accumulated after the next purge. Violates purge as 'the one removal verb' (commands.c:89) and SPEC's log-pair model (docs/SPEC.md:49-54).
ledger.remedy_hint: Call unlink_log_index_for_log(r.log_path) in the sweep branch, and/or extend sweep_logs to also collect *.log.idx files whose record is gone.

ledger.title: Unserialized read-modify-write of records loses concurrent updates (rename/save vs reaper mark-finished)
ledger.evidence: hold_mark_run_finished loads the record (record.c:979), mutates, and rewrites the whole file (record.c:1015); rename/save do the same (call.c:245→266, call.c:307→335). No file locking exists anywhere (no flock/lockf/F_SETLK in src/). The O_EXCL temp only serializes overlapping writes; interleaved load→write phases silently clobber: e.g. 'hold rename X keep' landing between the reaper's load and write is erased — the name AND the saved=true protection (call.c:263-264) are lost, so a call the user just saved becomes purgeable; conversely a rename that loaded state=running rewrites 'running' over the reaper's exited/exit_code/ended_at.
ledger.remedy_hint: Take a per-id advisory lock (flock on the reserve/record fd) around load-modify-write, or merge-on-write for the disjoint fields (name/saved vs exit metadata).

ledger.title: Name uniqueness is check-then-act with no reservation: concurrent starts/renames can mint duplicate names
ledger.evidence: SPEC pins names 'unique per store' (docs/SPEC.md:47-48). hold_generate_run_name_for_id checks run_name_exists_in_store (call.c:355, 382) but the name only becomes durable when the record is written much later (start.c:1408, after spawn); rename likewise checks (call.c:238) then writes (call.c:266). Two simultaneous 'hold --name foo ...' invocations, or a rename racing a start, both pass the directory scan and both persist name 'foo'. IDs get a two-phase .reserve protocol (start.c:197-209) but names have no equivalent, and resolution of a duplicated name then fails as ambiguous (call.c:75-77).
ledger.remedy_hint: Reserve names the way ids are reserved (e.g. names/<name>.reserve hardlink or O_EXCL marker held until the record lands), or re-verify uniqueness after the record write and retire the loser.

ledger.title: Public projection shape drifts from SPEC's allowlist, and store.md documents fields/files that no longer exist
ledger.evidence: SPEC.md:32-36 says the public entry 'carries only what every user may see: the call id, a generated name when present, an honest state hint, timestamps, and the ... ports'. hold_write_public_index_atomic additionally emits a full State object with Pid/Pgid/Sid/ExitCode/Error (record.c:451 → write_state_object record.c:83-108). Redaction of truly private data does hold — public Config is empty (record.c:112-114) and no argv/env/user is written; Error is never populated by any writer (only round-tripped: grep shows launch_error set nowhere outside load). Meanwhile docs/store.md:72 documents a 'requires_elevation' key the code does not write (record.c:420-452) and store.md:36-40 still shows profiles.json/aliases.json from the deleted profile/alias subsystems.
ledger.remedy_hint: Either amend SPEC's allowlist to include the Docker-shaped State (pids/exit code) as deliberate, or drop those fields from the public entry; refresh store.md to the current projection and layout.

ledger.title: Purge sweep deletes corrupt records silently and omits them from its accounting, contradicting SPEC's 'reported' invariant
ledger.evidence: SPEC.md:60-62: 'corrupt records are reported (and swept by purge), never silently obeyed'. cmd_prune_store_all unlinks any record that fails to load or validate with no stderr message and no stats update (list.c:681-688), unlike list which warns 'skipping corrupt record' (list.c:202, 206). A sweep that removes only corrupt records then prints 'hold: nothing to purge' (commands path list.c:866) after deleting files. The unlink also fires for a corrupt record of a still-running call, after which the orphan-log pass deletes its live log (list.c:748-752).
ledger.remedy_hint: Print the same 'corrupt record' warning, count removals in stats, and consider keeping the log when a swept corrupt record's pid/pgid still probes live.

ledger.title: Record validation never constrains log_path/console_sock to the store, so destructive verbs obey arbitrary paths from a record
ledger.evidence: hold_valid_record checks only pid>0, pgid>1 and id shape (validate.c:82-85); log_path/console_sock are accepted verbatim from JSON (record.c:624-628, 677-679; console_sock only requires a leading '/'). Purge then unlink()s those paths (list.c:640-646, 700-706) and redial appends to log_path (call.c:115-119 → start.c open_log_append_no_symlink, which O_NOFOLLOWs only the final components). Basis for judgment: store dirs are 0600/0700 so planting a record needs the victim's own privileges today, but SPEC's 'records are trusted only after validation' (SPEC.md:62) is the pinned defense-in-depth contract, and root purge of a system store would unlink wherever a (root-written) record points.
ledger.remedy_hint: During load or before unlink/append, require log_path/console_sock to resolve inside store->log_dir/console_dir (prefix check on the already-known store layout), else treat the record as corrupt.

ledger.title: hold_gen_id_for_store and its two collision helpers are dead code duplicating the live reservation protocol in start.c
ledger.evidence: hold_gen_id_for_store (layout.c:204-263) plus static id_collides_in_store/id_material_collides_in_store (layout.c:161-202) have no caller anywhere in src/ or tests — grep finds only the definition and the declaration at include/hold/store.h:14. The real id reservation is reserve_hashed_run_id in start.c:197-209 with its own collision check at start.c:154-155. Two divergent copies of the reserve/collision protocol invite drift (the dead one consults public stores and re-checks post-reserve; the live one differs), against the owner's no-ballooning/subtract-first workstyle.
ledger.remedy_hint: Delete hold_gen_id_for_store, the two static helpers, and the store.h declaration (~100 lines), or fold any still-wanted cross-store checks into reserve_hashed_run_id.

ledger.title: Console auto-detection stack from hold-on-identity.md is unimplemented except a race-prone one-shot TIOCGPGRP at detach time
ledger.evidence: hold-on-identity.md:163-175 specifies four detectors: tty_nr from /proc/<pid>/stat, termios dwell-time sampling on the broker poll loop (flap resistance), foreground-pgid tracking in the hold on proxy loop 'anchor[ing] classification at the spawn moment' with /proc/<fg>/exe snapshots 'when the child starts', and a confirmatory positioning-escape scan of the replay buffer. Grep over src/ finds none of these: no tty_nr use, no tcgetattr on the PTY master in broker.c, no escape scan in console/*, no session-type classification anywhere. The only artifact is a single TIOCGPGRP ioctl at shell.c:736, executed after the detach keys, with exe/cwd read at that same moment (shell.c:431,441) — not at spawn. Consequence of the missing spawn anchor: if the foreground program exits between the Ctrl-P Ctrl-Q keypress and the ioctl (or the user detaches at an idle prompt), fg_pgid == shell_pid and shell.c:410-413 silently SIGHUPs the wrapper shell and returns 0 — the whole guarded session ends with no adoption and no message. The doc carries a draft-for-redline banner, so this is direction-vs-code drift, verified unexecuted as instructed.
ledger.remedy_hint: Either implement the spawn-anchored fg-pgid tracking (sample TIOCGPGRP in the relay loop so the last stable non-shell foreground group is adopted) and stage the rest of the stack, or redline the identity doc to what v0.6.1 actually does.

ledger.title: docs/console.md documents deleted subsystems and dead verbs, with four broken links
ledger.evidence: console.md:50-52 has a whole 'Profiles and console mode' section (`hold start <profile> --console`) though the profile system is deleted (identity.md cut table; only vestiges like hold_valid_profile_hash in core/validate.c:51 remain); console.md:64-65,88,100 reference `hold dump`, which is not in the cli.c verb table (cli.c:25-43 registers on/off/tail/logs/attach/console/purge/prune — no dump); console.md:94-96 describe 'profile-labeled records', 'self-elevation' and 'profile capability paths' (elevation machinery deleted; src/access/ holds only invocation.c); console.md:3,112 link to quickstart.md, security.md, cli-contract.md, and target-resolution.md, none of which exist in docs/ (verified by ls). console.md is linked as a normative mechanism reference from SPEC.md:8.
ledger.remedy_hint: Rewrite console.md against the current surface (hold -t / hold attach / hold end, replay buffer, adopted mode) and drop the dead sections/links — a conserving subtraction.

ledger.title: hold on / hold off surface is pinned in the identity doc but has zero test coverage under those names; hold off is entirely untested
ledger.evidence: hold-on-identity.md:38-41 pins `hold on`/`hold off` as the guarded-shell surface, and cli.c:28-29,130-132 register both verbs. tests/test_hold.sh (135 tests) exercises the session only via the legacy `shell` alias (`"$HOLD_BIN" shell`, lines 3705, 3728, 3775) and `hold help shell` (line 3698); grep finds no test invoking `hold on`, `hold off`, or HOLD_ON_PID. The whole hold_cmd_off_action path — HOLD_ON_PID parsing, /proc comm cross-check, SIGTERM to the proxy, the proxy's g_hold_on_off_requested unwind (shell.c:727-733) — is unasserted, contrary to the owner's 'contracts pinned in docs + tests' discipline and the identity doc's 'every surface shape asserted'.
ledger.remedy_hint: Add shell tests driving `hold on` by name plus a `hold off` round trip (session ends rc 0, no run id created, terminal restored), and decide whether the undocumented `shell` alias should survive redline.

ledger.title: Guarded-shell relay flushes a pending Ctrl-P only on poll timeout, so continuous PTY output withholds the keystroke indefinitely
ledger.evidence: relay_shell_pty holds a typed 0x10 as pending_ctrl_p and forwards it only in the `ready == 0` branch (shell.c:634-641); there is no deadline check when poll keeps returning ready (master output). A user who presses Ctrl-P (e.g. shell previous-history) while any process in the session writes to the PTY at least every 500ms never gets the byte delivered until output pauses. attach.c solved this same problem correctly with a CLOCK_MONOTONIC deadline evaluated even when traffic flows (attach.c:169-189); the two detach implementations also disagree in that `hold on` hardcodes Ctrl-P Ctrl-Q while attach honors --detach-keys (attach.c:21-29).
ledger.remedy_hint: Port attach.c's deadline-tracked pending-detach handling into relay_shell_pty (or share the byte-scanner), which also unifies --detach-keys behavior across both loops.

ledger.title: Source filtering is fully built and tested but unreachable: nothing ever sets source_mask, and STDIN/PTY streams are unrepresentable
ledger.evidence: state.source_mask is only ever zero: no viewer key assigns it (the tty.c:1131-1238 key loop has no source-cycle binding; Ctrl-Y toggles only the display column, tty.c:1228-1230) and hold_cmd_view_action exposes no CLI flag for it (signal.c:758-800). The footer therefore always renders 'src:all' — frozen into a test assertion (tests/test_hold.sh:981 expects 'src:all'). Deeper: hold_logidx_record_stream can only return STDOUT or STDERR (logging.c:209-211 inspects one bit), so the HOLD_LOG_SRC_STDIN/PTY mask bits (include/hold/log_viewer.h:13-14) and the IN/PTY branches (filter.c:119-120, tty.c:690-691) are dead; PTY console captures are written as "stdout" (src/console/broker.c:408). The engine-level mask logic is exercised only by tests (viewer_filter_test.c:396-435), never by the product.
ledger.remedy_hint: Either wire a source-cycle key (and record PTY writes as PTY in the broker) or subtract the dead mask surface — mask bits, IN/PTY branches, and the 'src:' footer cluster — per the no-ballooning workstyle. Half-shipped is the worst of both.

ledger.title: Identity-doc surface `hold logs -p -t/--time/--date` does not exist; plain mode cannot render sidecar timestamps at all
ledger.evidence: hold-on-identity.md:63-66 specifies `-t/--time prepends times, --date adds date+time (timestamps read from the sidecar, never parsed out of the log text)`. The flag loop (signal.c:758-800) parses no such flags; probe: `hold logs <id> -p --time` and `-p -t` both exit 5 with the usage line. print_view_result (signal.c:458-480) never consults the sidecar, and hold_logidx_format_time plus HOLD_TS_* modes are reachable only inside the TTY viewer (tty.c:683). The doc carries the draft-for-redline banner, but this is the command-surface section (not the cut table), and it is the only specified way to get timestamps into script-consumable output.
ledger.remedy_hint: Implement -t/--time/--date in the plain path by prefixing hold_logidx_format_time output when an idx record exists at the line offset (blank prefix when it does not — never parse text), or redline the flags out of the identity doc.

ledger.title: The sidecar crash-recovery rule and truncation clamp are asserted in code comments but have zero test coverage
ledger.evidence: The load-time recovery rule — 'trust the smaller of the header count and the count physically present' (logging.c:265-271) — and the 1 MiB record-length clamp that sets HOLD_LOGIDX_META_TRUNCATED (logging.c:327-330) are untested: build_indexed_log (viewer_filter_test.c:325-348) only ever produces a healthy two-entry sidecar, and no shell test touches .log.idx contents (grep over tests/). My compiled probe confirmed the recovery behavior works today (torn third entry -> count 2; header overcount 9 -> count 2), but nothing pins it, so a regression in the durability rule that SPEC.md:50-53 leans on would ship silently.
ledger.remedy_hint: Add two cheap cases to viewer_filter_test.c: truncate the idx mid-entry and expect physical count; inflate header entry_count and expect physical count. Optionally one >1MiB write asserting the TRUNCATED meta bit.

ledger.title: The documented exit-code table (hold help scripting) disagrees with pinned behavior: usage/arity errors exit 5, not 1
ledger.evidence: help_scripting documents "1 usage / generic error" and "5 target not found or invalid target" (src/cli.c:100-107). But the parser returns 5 for pure usage errors: wrong arity prints the usage line and returns 5 (cli.c:205-208), missing flag values return 5 (cli_main.c:349-351, 358-360, 399-400), logs option misuse returns 5 (src/runtime/signal.c:761-799); meanwhile unknown flags on list/ps/purge return 1 (cli_main.c:633-639, 882-887). Both sides are pinned by tests, so this is a genuine docs-vs-tests contract split: tests/test_hold.sh:4052-4067 asserts rc=5 for `hold tail deadbeef extra` (an arity/usage error), while :820-825 and :2807-2812 assert rc=1 for unknown flags. Under the owner's "contracts pinned in docs + tests" workstyle, the two pins contradict each other: the same class of operator mistake (bad invocation shape) maps to 1 or 5 depending on which parser branch catches it.
ledger.remedy_hint: Since tests pin the codes, redline the help_scripting table to match reality (e.g. "5 target not found, invalid target, or malformed command line"), or unify usage errors on one code and update the two test sites.

ledger.title: Identity surface documents `hold logs` timestamp flags -t/--time and --date that no code implements
ledger.evidence: hold-on-identity.md:63-66 specifies for `hold logs <call> -p`: "-t/--time prepends times, --date adds date+time (timestamps read from the sidecar, never parsed out of the log text)". grep for "--time" and "--date" across all of src/ returns zero hits; the logs/__view option parser (src/runtime/signal.c:760-800) accepts only --follow/-f, --limit/--tail/-n, --plain/--print/-p, --interactive, --literal, --similar, --debug-stats, --help. An unrecognized `--date` after the target falls into the final else branch and exits 5 with usage (signal.c:796-799); `-t` before the verb is swallowed as the bare-form --tty flag (cli_main.c:127-130), so `hold -t logs x` becomes a console launch of the command "logs". The doc is "draft for redline" (line 3), so this is either an unexecuted direction item or an overpromise, but as written the identity surface names input the parser does not take; also the spec usage string (cli.c:35) and the inner usage (signal.c:761) disagree with each other about -n.
ledger.remedy_hint: Redline the doc to drop -t/--time/--date until implemented, or implement sidecar-timestamp prefixes in the plain dump path and pin them; align cli.c:35 and signal.c usage strings while there.

ledger.title: Current (non-archive) technical references still document deleted subsystems: `hold start <profile>`, self-elevation, profile capability paths, profiles.json
ledger.evidence: docs/index.md:32 lists console.md among current "Technical references" ("Historical... documentation for removed subsystems live in archive/", index.md:8-10). Yet docs/console.md:52 documents "hold start <profile>" and "hold start <profile> --console" — no `start` verb exists (command_specs, src/cli.c:25-49) and profiles are deleted (identity cut table line 186; only vestige in code is hold_valid_profile_hash used as an alias-shape guard, src/core/validate.c:51-70). console.md:96 documents "self-elevation through the same system target and profile capability paths as other privileged actions" — elevation is deleted and its opposite is now pinned in code comments (cli_main.c:728-730: "Delegated execution is gone... refused rather than re-run under sudo"). docs/store.md (also indexed as current) documents profiles.json/aliases.json layout (store.md:21,37), a requires_elevation public-index field (store.md:51,72) that grep shows no code writes (src/store/record.c:390-434 writes id/root_managed/state_hint/etc.), and links to docs/quickstart.md, profiles-and-aliases.md, security.md (store.md:3) which exist only under archive/.
ledger.remedy_hint: Rewrite console.md's launch examples in the current surface (hold --console / hold -it) and delete the elevation paragraph; refresh or archive store.md; fix its header links.

ledger.title: hold_resolve_binary_path treats a PATH directory as an executable match, so commands the shell runs fine fail under hold with a misleading error
ledger.evidence: paths.c:116-117 accepts any candidate with access(candidate, X_OK)==0 — true for traversable directories — without an S_ISREG check, unlike execvp which skips non-regular entries and continues the PATH scan. Probe: with PATH=$T/a:$T/b, a directory $T/a/prog and a real script $T/b/prog, the shell printed 'real-prog-ran' while `hold prog` failed: "hold: cannot start '/tmp/tmp.uXme4rTBj8/a/prog': Permission denied" — an error naming a path the user never typed, for a command that works everywhere else.
ledger.remedy_hint: stat the candidate and require S_ISREG (plus X_OK) before accepting, continuing the PATH scan otherwise — mirrors execvp.

ledger.title: macOS boot-id fallback derives from kern.boottime, which shifts on wall-clock steps; a live call can be misread as STALE and refuse end/stop
ledger.evidence: boot.c:18-24 synthesizes the boot id as 'macos-<sec>.<usec>' from sysctl kern.boottime. Basis: xnu recomputes boottime when the clock is set (settimeofday/NTP step), so the synthesized id is not stable within one boot. Consequences in current code: state.c:29-31 returns STATE_STALE on boot-id mismatch, and signal.c:107-110 then refuses to signal ('call %s is stale and cannot be signaled') — a running call could become un-endable via hold after a clock step. Linux uses the kernel's random boot_id (config.h:62) and is unaffected.
ledger.remedy_hint: On macOS round boottime to whole seconds and/or tolerate small deltas, or derive from a stabler token (e.g. kern.bootsessionuuid, available since 10.10).

ledger.title: User lookup parses /etc/passwd by hand only; on macOS (a supported platform) regular accounts live in Directory Services, so shell/USER-column/sudo-provenance lookups fail
ledger.evidence: hold_lookup_passwd_by_uid (paths.c:37-83) fopen's /etc/passwd and is the ONLY passwd mechanism in the tree — grep shows no getpwuid/getpwuid_r anywhere (pwd.h is included at config.h:24 but unused). Callers: shell.c:44 (login-shell fallback for 'hold on'), list.c:169 (USER column owner name), invocation.c:26-28 (SUDO_UID home resolution feeding hold_init_invoking_user_store). Basis for the macOS claim: standard macOS accounts (uid >= 501) are stored in Open Directory, not /etc/passwd, so these lookups return ENOENT for normal users; macOS is a first-class target per config.h:79-83 (/var/db/hold), boot.c and process.c __APPLE__ branches, and SPEC.md:19.
ledger.remedy_hint: Under __APPLE__ (where the static-link NSS concern does not apply) call getpwuid_r first and keep the file parse as the Linux-static fallback.

ledger.title: Dead prototype hold_sha256_file_hex has no definition anywhere; profile-era PROFILE_HASH_* vocabulary survives in core despite profiles being deleted
ledger.evidence: core.h:68 declares 'int hold_sha256_file_hex(const char *path, char out[PROFILE_HASH_STR_LEN])' but grep over src/, include/ and tests/ finds no definition or caller — any future call is a link error. Related leftovers: hold_valid_profile_hash is exported at core.h:22 yet its only use is internal to hold_valid_alias (validate.c:70), and PROFILE_HASH_HEX_LEN/PROFILE_HASH_STR_LEN persist at config.h:48-49 plus a types.h:7 comment, while hold-on-identity.md:136 states 'profiles do not exist' and the cut table marks the profile system deleted. sha256 otherwise remains correctly scoped: only run-id derivation (start.c:167-195) and the shell path (shell.c:343-385).
ledger.remedy_hint: Delete the dead prototype, fold hold_valid_profile_hash into a static is_full_hex helper inside validate.c, and rename the constants (it is the 64-hex-id length, ID_HEX_LEN already exists).

ledger.title: Two near-identical /proc/<pid>/stat parsers coexist in process.c
ledger.evidence: read_process_ids_state (process.c:42-108) and the Linux branch of hold_read_proc_stat_tokens (process.c:269-318) duplicate the same sequence: snprintf of /proc/%ld/stat into a 128-byte path, EINTR-looped read into a 4096 buffer, saved-errno close, strrchr(')')+2, strtok_r field walk with index bookkeeping. Only the extracted fields differ (state/pgid/sid vs state/starttime). ~70 duplicated lines of fiddly parsing that must be kept in sync (e.g. the comm-with-spaces handling and the EIO-on-empty-read convention appear twice).
ledger.remedy_hint: Extract one static read_stat_fields(pid, ...) that returns the post-')' token array (or takes wanted indices) and implement both public functions on it — pure subtraction.

ledger.title: Dead ID-generation cluster in layout.c: hold_gen_id_for_store plus both collision helpers and hold_chown_root_if_root (~120 lines) superseded by the hashed reservation paths
ledger.evidence: nm cross-reference over all release objects shows hold_gen_id_for_store and hold_chown_root_if_root defined in obj/store/layout.o and referenced by no other object; grep shows no call sites anywhere in src/ or tests/. Actual ID creation goes through reserve_hashed_run_id (start.c:197, called at start.c:893) and shell_hashed_adopt_id (shell.c). The statics id_collides_in_store (layout.c:161-182) and id_material_collides_in_store (layout.c:184-202) are called only by the dead hold_gen_id_for_store (layout.c:204-263). hold_chown_root_if_root is layout.c:13-21 with zero callers.
ledger.remedy_hint: Delete layout.c:10-11,13-21,161-263 and the two decls in include/hold/store.h:8,14-18. ~120 lines. Verified no test references any of these symbols.

ledger.title: Nine more link-verified dead exports across core/, cli.c, and paths.c (~140 lines), including one declaration with no definition at all
ledger.evidence: nm over all objects confirms zero external references and grep confirms zero intra-TU callers for: hold_sha256_file_hex (core.h:68 — declared, DEFINED NOWHERE; linking any caller would fail), hold_resolve_self_executable_path (paths.c:219-251, 33 lines; purge's sudo re-exec at cli_main.c:897 uses readlink("/proc/self/exe") inline instead), hold_open_unique_temp (fs.c:60-85, 26 lines; record.c:156-163 and 397-401 hand-roll O_EXCL temps instead), hold_fsync_dir_path (fs.c:237-248), hold_format_relative_age (util.c:178-196, 19 lines; list.c uses hold_format_duration_human for the contract's humanized CREATED), hold_write_json_log_entry_fd + hold_write_json_log_bytes_fd (logging.c:381-387, identical wrappers), hold_json_get_args_alloc (json.c:428-430), hold_valid_target_atom (validate.c:123-125), hold_cli_command_is_public (cli.c:186-188, byte-identical to the live hold_cli_command_is_parser_owned at cli.c:182-184). viewer_filter_test.c's symbol list (checked) uses none of them.
ledger.remedy_hint: Delete all nine definitions plus their header declarations (~140 lines with decls). None appears in tests/ — verified per symbol.

ledger.title: The identity doc's '--cap paths: delete' cut is unexecuted — ~190 lines of live capability machinery, pinned by tests but absent from the parity contract
ledger.evidence: hold-on-identity.md:187 lists "grants, sudoers pinning, elevation, --cap paths (src/access/) | delete — cruft". Current code still accepts --cap-add/--cap-drop (cli_main.c:397-427), applies real capability changes (start.c:568-678, apply_child_capabilities ~111 lines incl. the ENOTSUP fallback), persists cap_add/cap_drop into records (record.c:136-141, 292-299, 762-770), and replays them on redial (call.c:149-152), plus struct fields in types.h and runtime.h. Neither flag appears in docker-parity-contract.md:16-27. However tests/test_hold.sh:2521-2574 asserts CapEff after --cap-drop ALL --cap-add NET_BIND_SERVICE, so deletion requires removing those tests too — per guardrail this is a decision item, not a free deletion.

ledger.title: Recipe ports/volumes plumbing is write-never dead code (~60 lines): -p and -v are rejected at intake, so the fields can never be populated, yet they are validated, copied, persisted, reloaded, and redialed
ledger.evidence: cli_main.c:284,288-291 rejects -p/-P and -v/--volume honestly (per contract) and nothing ever appends to run->ports or run->volumes (grep: only free at cli_main.c:70-71 and pass-through at 93-96). Yet start.c:797-800,814-815 validates them, start.c:1357-1362 copies them into the record, record.c:282-291 serializes "ports"/"volumes" (guarded by count>0 — never true), record.c:754-761 reloads them via hold_json_get_ports_alloc/hold_json_get_volumes_alloc (json.c:437-443), and call.c:145-148 replays them on redial. Grep of tests/ finds no test asserting "ports"/"volumes" recipe keys. Reading them back only serves records written by deleted pre-cut versions — a legacy shim against the stated workstyle.
ledger.remedy_hint: Delete the ports/volumes recipe fields end-to-end (types.h, runtime.h, cli_main.c, start.c, record.c, call.c, the two json.c wrappers): ~60 lines. Keep the -p/-v rejections and the separate observed_ports projection, which are contract-pinned.

ledger.title: hold_decode_json_log_line is a legacy-format shim on every log read path — current Hold never writes JSON log lines, yet tail and the viewer strdup-decode every line through it
ledger.evidence: The only live writer is hold_write_indexed_log_bytes_fd (logging.c:346, raw bytes + sidecar; SPEC.md:48-54 documents raw+HLOGIDX as the format), and tests/test_hold.sh:874 asserts logs do NOT contain '"log"'. Yet hold_decode_json_log_line (logging.c:389-411) still probes each line for a {"log":...} wrapper, and is called per-line from signal.c:50 (inside decoded_log_stream, signal.c:35-88, ~54 lines existing only to line-buffer this decode) and filter.c:204,437 — a malloc+strdup per visible line in the viewer hot path. No test writes a JSON-format log fixture (grep of tests/).
ledger.remedy_hint: Delete hold_decode_json_log_line, the decoded_log_stream layer in signal.c, and the two filter.c decode detours; stream raw bytes directly. ~90-100 lines, plus per-line allocation removed from the viewer.

ledger.title: The /proc/<pid>/stat pgid/sid/state reader is implemented three times (~150 lines), and the shell.c copy is the least strict
ledger.evidence: Near-identical readers: hold_proc_read_ids (observe.c:45-100, exported in observe.h:22 but nm shows no external user — only observe.c:232 calls it), read_process_ids_state (process.c:42-110, validates tokens via parse_pid_token), and shell_read_proc_ids (shell.c:132-180, parses with bare strtol(tok, NULL, 10) — no error checking, unlike the other two). All three share the identical open/EINTR-read/strrchr(')')/strtok_r skeleton. shell.c:182+ (find_process_in_pgid) additionally re-implements the /proc pgid-membership walk that observe.c's for_each_group_pid already does.
ledger.remedy_hint: Host one reader in platform/process.c (layer-safe: runtime already depends on platform), export it, delete the observe.c and shell.c copies: ~100 lines saved and one consistent validation policy.

ledger.title: Hash-derived ID reservation is duplicated between start.c and shell.c, including twin field-hash helpers and a third store-collision probe
ledger.evidence: start.c:162-218 (hash_field, compute_run_hash, reserve_hashed_run_id) and shell.c:341-395 (shell_hash_field, shell_hashed_adopt_id) both build sha256 over key/value fields via hold_sha256_update_nul_field with the same exe/cwd/argc/argv[i]/counter shape, differing only in scope string and pid/pgid fields. shell_id_available (shell.c:316-340) re-implements the record/log/public existence probe that layout.c's id_collides_in_store (161-182) already encodes (itself dead per the layout.c finding).
ledger.remedy_hint: One exported keyed-field hasher plus one id-availability probe, parameterized by scope string and extra fields: ~50-60 lines saved and a single definition of what makes an ID 'taken'.

ledger.title: signal.c (906 lines) is four verbs in one file — signal delivery is barely a quarter of it; inspect and the logs/view dispatcher are complete seams
ledger.evidence: Function map: tail streaming (signal.c:35-239, incl. hold_tail_log_until_exit:173), signal proper (240-405, hold_do_signal_action:240, hold_cmd_signal_action:336), tail verb (406-447), view/logs plumbing (448-582 plus hold_cmd_view_action:741), and a self-contained inspect JSON emitter (583-740: write_file_as_json_array_object, build_stdio_object, write_inspect_with_stdio, hold_cmd_inspect_action). The inspect block (~158 lines) and view block (~300 lines) share nothing with signaling except the resolve helper.
ledger.remedy_hint: Split along the existing seams (inspect.c, view/tail into a logs-verbs file) or at minimum move inspect out; the stop/kill escalation with target revalidation (SPEC safety invariant) stays where tests pin it.

ledger.title: The one removal verb (purge/prune, ~360 lines) lives inside list.c — two unrelated responsibilities in a 967-line file
ledger.evidence: list.c:609-967 is entirely removal machinery: unlink_public_index (609), unlink_log_index_for_log (619), hold_prune_one_run (626), cmd_prune_store_all (662), hold_cmd_purge_action (829). The listing half (rows, humanized CREATED/STATUS per docker-parity-contract.md:53-58, print_collected_table:352 shared by list and ps) ends at ~608. The identity doc calls purge "the one removal verb" (hold-on-identity.md:76-83) — it deserves its own TU rather than riding in the ledger renderer.
ledger.remedy_hint: Move lines 609-967 to src/runtime/purge.c; hold_prune_one_run is already declared in runtime_internal.h so the seam is header-clean. Zero behavior change, and both files land under ~610 lines.

ledger.title: --env-file is a contract-asserted flag with zero asserting tests
ledger.evidence: docker-parity-contract.md:9 states 'Tests assert the shapes written here' and line 22 lists '--env-file load KEY=VALUE lines'. grep for 'env-file' across tests/ returns no hits in test_hold.sh or test_040_dockerish.sh; the only matches are the implementation itself (src/cli_main.c:296,319,373,383). Every other flag in the contract block (-d, -i, -t, -e, --name, --rm, --restart, --detach-keys) has at least one asserting test (e.g. test_hold.sh:3225, 3478, 3503, 2610).
ledger.remedy_hint: Add a test beside test_docker_shaped_cli_flags_and_rm covering --env-file happy path plus the invalid-entry rejection at src/cli_main.c:319 (exit code and 'invalid --env-file entry at path:line' message).

ledger.title: Identity surface specifies logs -t/--time and --date, which are neither implemented nor tested
ledger.evidence: hold-on-identity.md:63-66 specifies 'hold logs <call> -p ... -t/--time prepends times, --date adds date+time (timestamps read from the sidecar)'. grep for "--time"/"--date" across src/ finds nothing (the only '-t' parse is --tty at src/cli_main.c:127), and tests/ has zero hits. Runtime probe: `./hold logs <id> -t -p` and `--time -p` both fail with 'usage: hold logs <target> [--follow|-f] [--plain|--interactive]' rc=5. The sidecar timestamps the doc promises to surface are unreachable from the CLI.

ledger.title: Flagship verbs 'hold on'/'hold off' and the activation banner are only reachable through the legacy 'shell' alias in tests
ledger.evidence: All guarded-shell tests invoke the alias spelling: test_hold.sh:3705,3728,3775 pipe into '"$HOLD_BIN" shell', and 3698 checks 'help shell'. grep finds no test invoking 'hold on' or 'hold off', and no test asserts the documented banner (hold-on-identity.md:38-41). The banner exists at src/runtime/shell.c:705-706 and my isolated probe of `hold on` printed it exactly, with `hold off` outside a session refusing rc=1 ('not inside a hold on session') — also unasserted. A regression breaking the primary 'on'/'off' dispatch (src/cli_main.c:717,760,765) while keeping 'shell' would pass 135/135.
ledger.remedy_hint: Switch one shell test to the 'on' spelling, assert the exact 'Hold is now active...' stderr banner, and add a two-line test for 'hold off' outside a session (message + rc).

ledger.title: make test-040 and version-sync run only in local ci.sh; no GitHub workflow ever executes them
ledger.evidence: scripts/ci.sh:39 runs check_version_sync.sh and ci.sh:55 runs 'make test-040' (the release-personality hold-dynamic smoke: Makefile:66-67 builds WITHOUT -DHOLD_TESTING). grep across .github/ finds no test-040, test_040, or check_version_sync reference in ci.yml or release.yml; ci.yml build-and-test runs only 'make test' (ci.yml:27) and release.yml test job likewise (release.yml:52). ledger.title: test_040 uses fixed 0.3s sleeps where the main suite was hardened to poll, leaving a load flake
ledger.evidence: test_040_dockerish.sh:59 'sleep 0.3' then immediately redials named-smoke (line 60), and line 62 'sleep 0.3' then requires exactly 2 log lines (line 63). If the 0.1s child or its supervisor finish-write outlives 0.3s under load, redial targets a still-running call and the assertion fails. The main suite already solved exactly this class with polled helpers (record_ended_soon, test_hold.sh:521-529, used at 2296-2429 after the documented TERM-to-KILL hardening at 2348-2360); test_040 predates that discipline. Basis: pattern comparison within the repo plus the suite's own hardening history.
ledger.remedy_hint: Replace both fixed sleeps with a small poll on the record's '"state": "exited"' (the script already polls elsewhere, lines 83-89 and 101-104).

ledger.title: test_group_kill_children gates both of its assertions on one-shot 0.2s sleeps instead of polling
ledger.evidence: Line 599: 'sleep 0.2' then a single ps scan must find both background sleeps already forked ([ -n "$children" ] || return 1, line 601) — under load bash may not have spawned them yet.
