Line 603: 'sleep 0.2' then a single pid_dead_enough check per child (604-605); pid_dead_enough (532-539) does not poll, unlike pgid_terminated (541-553) which retries 40x. Similar one-shot patterns: line 1888 (sleep 0.2 then tail must show the finished log line). These are the residue of the sleep style the TERM-to-KILL hardening removed elsewhere.
ledger.remedy_hint: Poll for children appearing (retry the ps scan) and reuse pgid_terminated-style loops for their disappearance; convert line 1888 to a grep-with-retry.

ledger.title: macOS release-gating smoke has a fixed 0.2s sleep before its log assertion
ledger.evidence: test_macos_ci.sh:36-41: 'sleep 0.2' then 'hold logs -p' must already contain mac-smoke-line, one shot, no retry. This script gates release publishing (release.yml:206-209 runs it in build-macos, and release job needs build-macos at line 240), so a scheduler beat on a shared macOS runner can fail a tagged release. Note also the macOS lane never runs tests/test_hold.sh at all (only this smoke + viewer-filter + version + installer tests, lines 97-101), and docs/CI-PARITY.md describes only the two Linux lanes — the macOS lane's reduced coverage is undocumented.
ledger.remedy_hint: Wrap the logs check in a 2s retry loop like the ps-port poll in test_040_dockerish.sh:83-89, and add one CI-PARITY.md sentence stating what the macOS lane does and does not cover.

ledger.title: PORTS IPv6 '[::]' rendering and comma-separated multi-port shape are asserted nowhere
ledger.evidence: docker-parity-contract.md:62-63 asserts PORTS is 'Docker-formatted: 127.0.0.1:8080/tcp, [::]:80/tcp, comma-separated'. The public-index fixture at test_hold.sh:2749 deliberately carries both ports ('127.0.0.1:8080/tcp, [::]:9090/tcp'), but the assertion at 2755 greps only '127\\.0\\.0\\.1:8080/tcp'; grep for '9090' or '\\[::\\]' in assertions returns nothing. The live-listener test (3362-3379) binds a single IPv4 socket, so neither the IPv6 bracket form nor the comma join is ever checked against the contract's exact shape.
ledger.remedy_hint: Extend the 2755 grep to match the full '127.0.0.1:8080/tcp, [::]:9090/tcp' cell, and/or bind a dual-stack pair in the live ports test.

ledger.title: hold-on-identity.md still carries the 'draft for redline' banner although three docs and the code already treat it as the approved contract
ledger.evidence: hold-on-identity.md:3-5: 'Status: draft for redline. When approved, this document sits above docker-parity-contract.md...'. The 'when approved' condition is already exercised: docker-parity-contract.md:5 declares itself 'normative, subordinate to hold-on-identity.md'; SPEC.md:5 says 'The command surface is specified by hold-on-identity.md'; index.md:14-16 files it under 'The contract'. Code-side, the cut table is essentially executed (run verb gone, profiles gone, captive.c absent, src/runtime has no grants/elevation, shell folded onto on at cli_main.c:717, viewer in core as hold logs).
ledger.remedy_hint: Redline complete: replace the banner with 'Status: normative' (noting the remaining unexecuted items, e.g. logs -t/--time/--date and the --cap paths, or executing them first).

ledger.title: Identity contract promises hold logs -p timestamp flags (-t/--time, --date); the binary rejects both
ledger.evidence: hold-on-identity.md:64-66: '-t/--time prepends times, --date adds date+time (timestamps read from the sidecar, never parsed out of the log text)'. Probe: `./hold logs -p -t <id>` and `./hold logs -p --date <id>` both print 'usage: hold logs <target> [--follow|-f] [--plain|--interactive]' and exit 5; the parser at src/runtime/signal.c:758-800 has no -t/--time/--date branch. This is the one row of the identity verb surface I found unimplemented (list scopes, ps -a, purge scopes/aliases, on/off, redial, save/rename all verified present).
ledger.remedy_hint: Implement -t/--time/--date from the HLOGIDX sidecar (the data is already there) with a shape test, or strike the clause from the contract.

ledger.title: Legacy verbs 'shell' and 'console' remain accepted aliases that no current document sanctions
ledger.evidence: src/cli.c:30 registers {"shell"...} and :40 {"console"...}; cli_main.c:717-719 folds shell->on and console->attach; the in-binary help admits them (cli.c:130 '(shell is an alias of on.)', cli.c:142 '(console is an alias of attach.)'). But hold --help, README, docker-parity-contract, and the identity surface (hold-on-identity.md:37-84) list neither, and the cut table (hold-on-identity.md:190) says 'hold shell | becomes hold on / hold off'. Identity sanctions only stop and rm/prune/drop as aliases (tail is sanctioned by README:126). Owner workstyle: no legacy shims.
ledger.remedy_hint: Either delete the shell/console spec rows and canonicalization (finishing the cut) or add them to the identity doc's sanctioned-alias list; pin whichever with a test.

ledger.title: hold list -h and hold ps -h fail with 'error: unknown flag' despite --help promising 'hold <command> -h  help for one command'
ledger.evidence: commands.c:94 prints 'hold <command> -h  help for one command'. Probe across all verbs: attach/end/kill/logs/inspect/ports/stats/save/rename/purge/on/off all exit 0 with help on -h, but `hold list -h` and `hold ps -h` print 'hold: error: unknown flag '-h'' plus usage and exit 1 (their flags go through the shared parser at cli_main.c:634 which has no -h case, unlike e.g. signal.c:792). Related intake nit: the misuse usage for purge (cli.c:41 'usage: hold purge [<target>] [-a|--all] [--force]') omits the -s/-u scope flags that exist and that `hold help purge` (cli.c:146) documents.
ledger.remedy_hint: Special-case -h/--help in the shared flag loop to route to help_action(canon), and add -s/-u to the purge/rm spec usage strings.

ledger.title: CONTRIBUTING.md pins the test contract to deleted profile-era artifacts: tests/profile_hash_vector.c does not exist and test_grant_* patterns are gone
ledger.evidence: CONTRIBUTING.md:36-37: 'tests/profile_hash_vector.c locks the profile-hash capability key' — tests/ contains only viewer_filter_test.c as a C file (ls verified); :12 advertises 'the regression suite + profile-hash vector' as part of make ci; :45 lists 'the profile hash' as a security-sensitive area; :47 points to 'test_grant_*' patterns — zero matches in tests/test_hold.sh (test_system_store_* and *_sudo_provenance do exist). scripts/ci.sh:5 and :52 carry the same stale 'profile-hash vector' step label while actually running make test + make test-040. The build/test commands themselves (make ci/test/lint, Makefile:55/66/92/96) do work as documented.
ledger.remedy_hint: Strip the profile-hash and grant references from CONTRIBUTING.md and relabel the ci.sh step; point the security-test exemplar list at patterns that exist.

ledger.title: '-e KEY' without '=' is rejected instead of passing the host value through as docker run does
ledger.evidence: append_env_assignment requires an '=' (cli_main.c:246-250); probe: 'FOO=hostval ./hold -e FOO -d /bin/sleep 0.2' -> rc 5, 'hold: error: expected KEY=VALUE after --env/-e'. docker run -e KEY copies KEY from the client environment (and omits it when unset); docker-parity-contract.md:21 pins -e to docker run behavior. Same gap applies to --env-file lines via the shared parser. Also unaccepted: docker's combined '-dt'/'-di' bundles (parse_docker_run_flag, cli_main.c:113-141, handles only -it/-ti).
ledger.remedy_hint: On bare KEY, look up getenv(KEY) and record KEY=value (skip when unset); optionally accept -dt/-di/-dit bundles or reject them with a targeted message.

ledger.title: VERIFIED: exactly one sudo/elevation re-exec site in all of src/, matching SPEC's 'single commented dispatch site' claim
ledger.evidence: grep over src/ for execv/execvp/execve/execl/fexecve returns 7 sites: cli_main.c:909 `execvp("sudo", sudo_argv)` (non-root `hold purge --system` only, gated at cli_main.c:895, with the auditability comment at 891-894); start.c:351 and start.c:1191 (execv of the held command), broker.c:228-230 (console child), shell.c:124-125 (user's shell). Case-insensitive grep for doas/pkexec/setuid/setresuid/"su" finds no other elevation mechanism; no system() or posix_spawn anywhere. SPEC.md:65-67's claim ('Hold never elevates ... one deliberate exception ... single commented dispatch site') is accurate against current code.
ledger.remedy_hint: None needed; this is a confirming verification the charter demanded.
ledger.failure_scenario: N/A — no defect; recorded so the crucible has the grep evidence that the SPEC claim holds.

ledger.title: /proc/net address decoding assumes a little-endian host; PORTS output is byte-reversed on big-endian Linux
ledger.evidence: observe.c:318-325 ipv4_from_proc_hex: `v = strtoul(hex,...,16)` then prints `v & 0xff` as the first octet. The kernel prints the __be32 with %08X of the native load, so '0100007F' (LE) decodes correctly to 127.0.0.1, but on a big-endian host the kernel emits '7F000001' and this code renders 1.0.0.127. ipv6_from_proc_hex (observe.c:329-351) hard-codes the same per-word LE byte extraction. The docker-parity-contract.md:62-63 pins the PORTS rendering ('127.0.0.1:8080/tcp').
ledger.remedy_hint: Decode via ntohl()/byte-order-aware reassembly (or document Linux-LE-only support); one-line change per function.
ledger.failure_scenario: On s390x/ppc64 (BE) Linux, `hold ports` and the PORTS column print reversed addresses (1.0.0.127 for a loopback listener), quietly violating the pinned Docker-format contract.

ledger.title: Observation surfaces (stats stream loop, ports) re-match membership by raw (pgid,sid) each sample with no identity re-check, so a recycled pair displays foreign process data under the call's name
ledger.evidence: stats.c:116 runs hold_eval_state (identity-checked) once, then the stream loop at stats.c:158-175 calls sample_group -> hold_observe_run_pids -> for_each_group_pid (observe.c:216-244), which matches only `pgid != r->pgid || sid != r->sid || state == 'Z'` — no starttime/exe token consulted on any subsequent iteration of an unbounded watch. ports.c:42-52 has the same eval-then-scan gap, one-shot. Observation only — no signal is sent from these paths.
ledger.remedy_hint: In the stream loop, break when hold_eval_state stops returning STATE_RUNNING (the callback already exists for the log viewer: interactive_view_run_is_running, signal.c:493) instead of relying solely on an empty sample.
ledger.failure_scenario: During a long `hold stats` watch the call dies and (pgid,sid) is recycled between 1s samples; the display silently switches to reporting an unrelated group's CPU/RSS/PIDS under the held call's id and name — misleading on a tool whose identity doc stresses honest observation.

ledger.title: hold off SIGTERMs the pid from HOLD_ON_PID with only a comm-name match (Linux) or a bare kill(pid,0) probe (non-Linux) — no starttime identity, plus a comm-read-to-kill TOCTOU
ledger.evidence: shell.c:755-787: pid comes from the HOLD_ON_PID env var; the Linux guard (shell.c:768-777) compares /proc/<pid>/comm against the caller's own comm ('hold'), then `kill((pid_t)pid, SIGTERM)` at shell.c:784. Any same-uid process whose comm is 'hold' passes, and the target can be recycled between read_proc_comm and kill. The non-Linux branch (shell.c:779-782) accepts any signalable pid. Contrast with the record-based path's starttime/exe revalidation (signal.c:112-148) — this is the one signal-sending path in the runtime that carries no start-time identity at all.
ledger.remedy_hint: Have `hold on` also export the proxy's /proc starttime (e.g. HOLD_ON_PID_STARTTIME) and require it to match before signaling; keeps the check two lines and closes both the recycle and the comm-collision cases.
ledger.failure_scenario: A stale HOLD_ON_PID survives in a subshell after the hold-on proxy exits; the pid is recycled by another of the user's hold processes (comm 'hold' matches); `hold off` SIGTERMs that unrelated hold invocation.

ledger.title: hold ports reports EACCES only when zero ports were found; partial permission-denied scans print an incomplete list with no note
ledger.evidence: ports.c:60-68: `if (denied && ports.count == 0) { ... return 3; } else { print ports }` — when some group members' /proc/<pid>/fd dirs are unreadable (socket_inode_cb sets *denied at observe.c:297) but at least one port was resolved from readable members, the denied flag is dropped and the truncated list prints as if complete, on a surface the parity contract defines as 'sockets actually observed in use by the call's process group' (docker-parity-contract.md:62).
ledger.remedy_hint: When denied && ports.count > 0, emit a one-line stderr note ('some processes in the group were unreadable; list may be incomplete') — stdout machine data stays clean per the output discipline.
ledger.failure_scenario: A call's group contains a setuid child holding the only listener; `hold ports` exits 0 with the other members' ports (or none of the interesting ones) and no hint that the scan was blocked, and a script concludes the service is not listening.

ledger.title: List reports a concurrently-purged record as 'corrupt': ENOENT is conflated with corruption
ledger.evidence: collect rows does hold_load_record(path,...) != 0 → 'hold: warning: skipping corrupt record %s' (list.c:201-203). hold_load_record returns -1 for any open failure including ENOENT (record.c:528-530 via fs.c:171-174), so a purge racing a list (readdir saw the entry, unlink landed before open) makes list warn about a corrupt record that was simply removed — misleading given SPEC reserves that report for real corruption.
ledger.remedy_hint: Skip silently when errno==ENOENT after the failed load; keep the corrupt warning for records that exist but fail to parse/validate.

ledger.title: String parser admits C0 control characters via \u escapes, letting ESC bytes round-trip into terminal-rendered fields
ledger.evidence: hold_parse_json_string rejects raw bytes <0x20 (json.c:131) and rejects only � and surrogates among escapes (json.c:105-106), so  decodes to a literal ESC byte. hold_json_escape writes control chars as \u00xx (json.c:24-25), so an argv containing ESC round-trips through the record into cmdline and is printed unfiltered in the list COMMAND column (list.c:230; hold_append_cmd_human copies bytes verbatim, util.c:102-117). Parser limits are otherwise sound: JSON_MAX_DEPTH 64 (json.c:200), MAX_RECORD_BYTES 1MiB (fs.c:188), strtoll/strtoull overflow checked (json.c:269, 306), pid/uid/gid narrowing checked (record.c:487-523).
ledger.remedy_hint: Either reject - in hold_parse_json_string (records legitimately never need them unescaped) or strip/replace control bytes at render time in the human formatters.

ledger.title: Adopted-run id availability skips the console-socket check that launched runs perform, while the listener silently steals any existing socket
ledger.evidence: shell_id_available (shell.c:316-340) checks record, log, and public paths but not `<console_dir>/<id>.sock`, whereas the launched-run availability check does include it (start.c:156-157, store/layout.c:172-173,197-198). hold_make_console_listener then unconditionally unlink()s any pre-existing socket at the path before bind (socket.c:103), so a colliding adopted id would silently steal a live broker's socket instead of being skipped. Practically remote (SHA-256 over pid/pgid/start_ns, shell.c:361-390), but it is an inconsistency between the two id-availability paths guarding the same invariant.
ledger.remedy_hint: Add the console-sock existence probe to shell_id_available, mirroring start.c:156.

ledger.title: Detach-key matcher cannot detach when the typed stream overlaps a failed prefix of a custom sequence
ledger.evidence: On mismatch, hold_console_process_interactive_byte pops only the current byte, forwards the entire older pending prefix, and re-tests just the current byte (attach.c:72-76) — no failure-function/suffix retention. With `--detach-keys a,a,b` (accepted by cli_main.c:187-243), typing 'aaab' forwards 'aa', re-arms on the third 'a', then fails 'ab' and forwards both — the trailing 'aab' never matches, so detach is impossible for sequences with repeated prefixes unless typing pauses. The default ctrl-p,ctrl-q has no repeated prefix and is unaffected.
ledger.remedy_hint: On mismatch, retry matching from the longest pending suffix (tiny KMP-style fallback over the <=8-byte buffer) instead of only the current byte.

ledger.title: Dead misnamed API: hold_write_json_log_entry_fd / hold_write_json_log_bytes_fd have no callers and no longer write JSON
ledger.evidence: Both functions (logging.c:381-387, declared in include/hold/core.h:119-120) just forward to hold_write_indexed_log_bytes_fd with idx_fd=-1. Repo-wide grep finds no call sites in src/ or tests/. The 'json' names are leftovers from the pre-0.4 JSON log format (docs/archive/0.4-release-cut.md:59 records the raw+sidecar switch) and misleadingly imply the store still contains JSON logs, which props up the decode shim's apparent legitimacy.
ledger.remedy_hint: Delete both wrappers and their header declarations in the same change that removes hold_decode_json_log_line.

ledger.title: Capture hot path pays ~5 extra syscalls per log line: header pread + full 80-byte header pwrite + fstat on every record
ledger.evidence: append_logidx_entry re-reads the header (pread, logging.c:323), writes the 16-byte entry, then rewrites the entire 80-byte header (logging.c:342) for every line; hold_write_indexed_log_bytes_fd additionally does lseek+fstat per line segment (logging.c:353-360, 367-374). Basis: read of the writer path; the per-line header rewrite also widens the crash window in which a torn header makes hold_open_log_index_fd discard the whole sidecar via ftruncate(0) (logging.c:187-196), losing all prior timestamps. Durability of the count-commit rule needs the header update, but the count could be cached in memory by the single writer and the header reread eliminated.
ledger.remedy_hint: Cache the header in the writer (single-writer invariant already holds per capture process) and pwrite only the 24-byte count/size/mtime span; keep the entry-then-header ordering that the min-count recovery rule relies on.

ledger.title: Bare ESC blocks the viewer waiting for a follow-up byte, and any Alt-modified key quits it
ledger.evidence: read_key on 0x1b calls blocking read_byte for the next byte (tty.c:210-212): pressing plain ESC freezes input handling (and the 250ms follow tick loop) until another key arrives, since VMIN=1/VTIME=0 (tty.c:156-157). If the next byte is neither 'O' nor '[' the viewer returns VIEWER_KEY_QUIT (tty.c:222), so terminal Alt-<letter> sequences (ESC+char) silently exit the polished viewer — at odds with the quiet, no-surprise chrome standard (hold-on-identity.md:26-28).
ledger.remedy_hint: After ESC, poll stdin with a ~25ms timeout: no byte means bare ESC (map to help-close/no-op), and treat unrecognized ESC-prefixed sequences as VIEWER_KEY_NONE rather than QUIT.

ledger.title: Purge-family usage strings drift from the flags actually accepted (-s/-u omitted everywhere; rm's line also omits -a)
ledger.evidence: The one-line usage shown on arity errors says "usage: hold purge [<target>] [-a|--all] [--force]" for purge/prune/drop (cli.c:42,43,45) and "usage: hold purge [<target>] [--force]" for rm (cli.c:44), while all four accept -s/--system and -u/--user (purges => scoped, cli_main.c:579-616) and rm accepts -a/--all like its siblings (HOLD_CLI_ALLOW_ALL on cli.c:44; sweeps includes purges at cli_main.c:595-603). The full help topic gets it right (cli.c:147-152 shows [-a|--all] [-s|--system] [-u|--user] [--force]), matching hold-on-identity.md:76-83. So the string users see at the moment of a mistake advertises a narrower surface than both the accepted flags and the contract.
ledger.remedy_hint: Make the four command_specs usage strings identical and complete: usage: hold purge [<target>] [-a|--all] [-s|--system] [-u|--user] [--force].

ledger.title: hold_cli_command_is_parser_owned and hold_cli_command_is_public are byte-identical duplicates
ledger.evidence: src/cli.c:182-188: both functions are `return find_public_command_spec(s) != NULL;`. Two names for one predicate invite divergence and pad the public header surface (both declared in include/hold/cli.h). Under the owner's subtract/consolidate preference this is a free deletion: keep one name, drop the other.
ledger.remedy_hint: Delete one (grep callers; keep hold_cli_command_is_parser_owned, which cli_main.c:559 uses) and remove the dead declaration from include/hold/cli.h.

ledger.title: Live verb surface exceeds the identity doc: tail verb, shell/console aliases, and leading -f/--tail launch flag are test-pinned but absent from the redline surface
ledger.evidence: hold-on-identity.md's surface block (lines 37-84) lists no `tail`, `shell`, or `console` verbs, and its cut table says `hold shell` "becomes" hold on/off (line 190). Code keeps all three: tail (cli.c:34, dispatched cli_main.c:850-853, usage pinned by tests/test_hold.sh:4055), shell folded onto on (cli.c:30, cli_main.c:717, help discloses "shell is an alias of on" cli.c:131), console folded onto attach (cli.c:41, cli_main.c:719, usage pinned test_hold.sh:4059). The leading bare-form flags -f/--tail (cli_main.c:484-487) and --console (489-493) appear in no current doc's flag list (docker-parity-contract.md:16-27 omits them; --console survives only in console.md, -f/--tail only in docs/archive/cli-contract.md:41). Since the identity doc is draft-for-redline direction, this is drift to resolve at redline time rather than a violation, but tests currently pin surface the contract does not name.
ledger.remedy_hint: At redline, either add tail/shell/console to the identity surface as documented aliases (one line each) or execute the cut and update the pinned usage tests; give -f/--tail and --console a home in the parity contract or remove them.

ledger.title: hold_read_small_file is a one-line alias of hold_read_owned_file_no_symlink whose bland name hides the ownership/symlink policy callers rely on
ledger.evidence: fs.c:249-251 is 'return hold_read_owned_file_no_symlink(path, out);' — nothing else. Two callers (record.c:804, signal.c:661) plus the core.h:66 declaration. The alias name suggests a plain bounded read, but the callee enforces O_NOFOLLOW, an euid ownership check (fs.c:183), and the MAX_RECORD_BYTES cap — safety properties a reader at the call site cannot see under the 'small file' name.
ledger.remedy_hint: Delete the wrapper and call hold_read_owned_file_no_symlink directly at both sites; the explicit name documents the invariant.

ledger.title: Core helpers are reimplemented inline at higher layers: filename→id parsing in resolve.c and hex encoding in layout.c
ledger.evidence: resolve.c:206-212 (suffix check, len bounds, memcpy, NUL, hold_valid_id) is exactly hold_record_json_filename_id (validate.c:21-33), which the same file already uses at resolve.c:54 and :168. layout.c:219-221 hex-encodes random id bytes with a snprintf("%02x") loop although hold_hex_encode (sha256.c:90-101) exists and is used elsewhere (fs.c:71, start.c:194). Same-behavior duplicates that can drift from the validated core versions.
ledger.remedy_hint: Replace both inline blocks with the existing core helpers (direction is fine: runtime/store may call core per lint_layers.sh).

ledger.title: hold_group_exists lacks the pgid<=1 guard its sibling has; kill(-1,0)/kill(0,0) would misreport 'group exists' for degenerate pgids
ledger.evidence: process.c:369-377 calls kill(-pgid, 0) unguarded: pgid==1 probes every process (kill(-1,0) succeeds → returns 1) and pgid==0 probes the caller's own group. hold_group_session_liveness guards exactly this at process.c:112-114 (pgid<=1 → GROUP_SCAN_ERROR). Currently unreachable-in-anger: state.c:26 returns STATE_UNKNOWN before the :70 call for pgid<=1, and target_group_gone (state.c:97) runs only after validate_signal_target rejects pgid<=1 (signal.c:96-99) — but the asymmetry leaves the sole guard at distant call sites for a probe whose false positive stalls stop/kill wait loops.
ledger.remedy_hint: Mirror the sibling: return -1 (or 0) for pgid<=1 inside hold_group_exists — one-line hardening consistent with the safety invariant.

ledger.title: hold_rand_bytes opens /dev/urandom without O_CLOEXEC, breaking the file's otherwise uniform close-on-exec discipline
ledger.evidence: sha256.c:127 is 'open("/dev/urandom", O_RDONLY)' with no O_CLOEXEC and no EINTR retry, while every other open in core/platform passes O_CLOEXEC (fs.c:6,35,75,96,171,238; process.c:47,273). The fd is closed on all paths before return (sha256.c:140,143), so leakage requires a concurrent fork — hold forks in start.c — making this a narrow-window inconsistency rather than a live leak; config.h:64-66 even defines an O_CLOEXEC fallback so the flag is free.
ledger.remedy_hint: Add O_CLOEXEC (and an EINTR retry) to match the rest of the codebase.

ledger.title: hold_parse_rfc3339_utc_to_ns silently accepts non-UTC offsets and arbitrary trailing garbage as UTC
ledger.evidence: util.c:250-268 strptime's only '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S' and ignores everything after seconds, so '2026-07-03T01:23:45+05:00' or '...45garbage' parse as a UTC instant (the comment at util.c:247-249 admits only 'fractional seconds and the trailing Z ignored'). timegm then misplaces offset timestamps by the offset. Blast radius is small today — callers are display ordering only (list.c:115, :315, :327) and hold itself always writes 'Z' — but SPEC.md:50-53 pins the record as a stable format others may write.
ledger.remedy_hint: After strptime, require end=='Z'||'\0' (optionally consuming '.fraction'), returning false otherwise — constrains the accepted grammar to what the comment claims.

ledger.title: Seven exported symbols are used only inside their defining TU — externalized statics inflating the public headers
ledger.evidence: nm shows no external referent for: hold_do_signal_action (runtime_internal.h:14; called only from signal.c:390), hold_record_matches_alias_intent (runtime_internal.h:17; only resolve.c:128), hold_proc_read_ids (observe.h:22; only observe.c:232), hold_sha256_update (core.h:32; only sha256.c:148,154), hold_skip_ws, hold_skip_json_value (core.h:45,47; json.c-internal), hold_valid_profile_hash (core.h:22; only validate.c:70). Guardrail exception: hold_parse_json_string is also externally dead in the release link but tests/viewer_filter_test.c calls it and Makefile:63 compiles json.c into that test — making it static would break the test link, so it must keep external linkage (or the test adapts).
ledger.remedy_hint: Make the first six static and drop ~7 header declaration lines (hold_proc_read_ids folds into the /proc-reader consolidation instead); leave hold_parse_json_string exported or restructure the unit test first.

ledger.title: Stale post-cut vocabulary: docker_run's comment cites the deleted `run` verb, profile-era names and macros linger, and two helper twins duplicate trivially
ledger.evidence: types.h:109 comment says "invoked via the Docker-shaped `run` verb" but no run verb exists (cli_main.c:701 sets it for any bare launch; grep '"run"' in cli_main.c/cli.c is empty). hold_valid_profile_hash (validate.c:51-61) is byte-equivalent to hold_valid_id (validate.c:5-19; PROFILE_HASH_HEX_LEN == ID_HEX_LEN == 64 in config.h:48,46) and survives only to reject 64-hex call names at validate.c:70; the PROFILE_HASH_* macros (config.h:48-49) exist only for it and the undefined hold_sha256_file_hex. Also: hold_read_small_file (fs.c:250-252) is a bare alias of hold_read_owned_file_no_symlink with callers split across both names (record.c:528 vs 804, signal.c:661), and hold_parse_uid_env/hold_parse_gid_env (validate.c:87-121) are 17-line identical twins.
ledger.remedy_hint: Reword the docker_run comment to 'bare launch form'; replace hold_valid_profile_hash with hold_valid_id and drop the PROFILE_* macros; pick one file-read name; fold the uid/gid parsers into one unsigned parser. ~40 lines of vocabulary debt.

ledger.title: list -u/--user, ps scope-flag rejection, and the 'drop' purge alias have zero test coverage
ledger.evidence: Identity doc: 'hold list -u ... your personal calls only, even under sudo' (line 127) and 'ps has no scope flags — it takes only -a' (105). grep finds no test invoking 'list -u'/'--user' (only list -l at test_hold.sh:4046) and none feeding ps a scope flag; the 'drop' alias (src/cli_main.c:579,721) is never invoked (prune and rm are, e.g. 3273). Probes confirm today's behavior is correct ('hold ps -s' -> "error: unknown flag '-s'" rc=1; 'hold list -u' renders the USER-column ledger), so these are unpinned correct behaviors, including the security-relevant sudo semantics of -u.
ledger.remedy_hint: One test asserting ps rejects -s/-u/-l with the usage line, one exercising list -u under the as_sudo_from_user helper, one line swapping an existing prune call for drop.

ledger.title: Shellcheck triage: all SC1087 hits are false positives (34 sites, not 10); SC2034 flags three genuinely dead locals
ledger.evidence: shellcheck 0.8.0 run this session reports SC1087 at 34 sites (the charter's 573,575,640,649,1831,1862,1863,1879,1945,1993 plus 2714-3689) — every one is "^$id[[:space:]]" inside a double-quoted regex, where bash expands $id then treats '[' literally; no array expansion can occur, so behavior is correct and no assertion is weakened. SC2034 is real but inert dead code: 'oldboot' and 'stale_id' declared at 1819 are never used (the function uses bootfile/stale_log and does assert staleness at 1831), and 'newname' at 2367 is unused ('keptsafe' is hardcoded at 2372). No masked assertions found behind any of the three.
ledger.remedy_hint: Brace the expansions (${id}[[:space:]]) repo-wide to silence SC1087, delete the three dead locals; consider a shellcheck step in ci.sh so the count cannot regrow silently.

ledger.title: Release installer smoke runs the identical --version check twice under two names
ledger.evidence: release.yml:295-298: installed_version and hold_version are both assigned from the same command '"$install_tmp/bin/hold" --version' and both compared to RELEASE_TAG — a copy-paste duplicate that reads as if a second surface (PATH copy or HOLD_ENV_FILE handoff) were verified when it is not. Relatedly, install.sh's env-file handoff (write_env_file, install.sh:325-334) and the uninstaller's leftover blank separator line (installer writes '\n'+marker+export at 362-365; uninstall awk at 448-452 removes only marker+1 line) have no CI or test coverage; tests/test_release_installer.sh:154-186 otherwise covers the uninstall round-trip well.
ledger.remedy_hint: Point the second check at a genuinely different surface (e.g. HOLD_ENV_FILE handoff sourcing) or delete it; optionally make the uninstall awk also eat the preceding blank line.

ledger.title: The viewer design doc is shipped work still filed under future/ as 'not current behavior'
ledger.evidence: viewer-fixes-before-0.5.md:5: 'Status: near-future work before 0.5.0. This is the target design, not a claim about current behavior', and future/README.md:5-7 says these notes 'should not be read as implemented features'. But v0.6.1 ships the viewer (src/viewer/ = 1,764 lines; CHANGELOG 0.6.0: 'full-screen viewer follows live calls by default (FOLLOWING ACTIVE), opens at the newest output'), and hold-on-identity.md:26-30 holds `hold logs` to 'the before-0.5 design's standard' — making an allegedly non-current future note the viewer's de-facto reference standard.
ledger.remedy_hint: Move the doc out of future/ (e.g. docs/viewer.md or archive/ once its content is absorbed into a current viewer reference) and update its status line plus the two index links.

ledger.title: Two user-facing error strings still say 'run record' where the identity contract mandates 'call' vocabulary
ledger.evidence: signal.c:734 hold_die_errno("hold: failed to inspect run record") and shell.c:575 hold_die_errno("hold: failed to write adopted run record") are the only reachable-string hits for the banned nouns (hold-on-identity.md:19: '"Run" and "container" do not appear in the vocabulary'); cli_main.c:731's 're-run as root' is verb use and fine. All other printf/sig_note/die strings grep clean for run/profile/container.
ledger.remedy_hint: s/run record/call record/ in both strings.

plays.thesis: Delete the link-verified dead-export cluster and the write-never ports/volumes recipe plumbing: ~320 lines of code that nothing calls, nothing tests, and one declaration (hold_sha256_file_hex) that has no definition anywhere and would fail the link if ever used.
plays.improves: Learnability and the <10k-line identity direction. Concretely: (1) layout.c dead ID-generation cluster — hold_gen_id_for_store (layout.c:204-263), its two static collision helpers (161-202), hold_chown_root_if_root (13-21), plus store.h:8,14-18 decls; grep this session shows zero callers, the live reservation protocol is reserve_hashed_run_id in start.c. (2) Seven dead exports with only definition+declaration hits: hold_sha256_file_hex (core.h:68, DEFINED NOWHERE), hold_resolve_self_executable_path (paths.c:219, platform.h:27), hold_open_unique_temp (fs.c:60), hold_fsync_dir_path (fs.c:237), hold_format_relative_age (util.c:178), hold_json_get_args_alloc (json.c:428), hold_valid_target_atom (validate.c:123), hold_cli_command_is_public (cli.c:186, cli.h:10). (3) The ports/volumes recipe fields that can never be populated — cli_main.c:284/290 reject -p/-v honestly and nothing appends to run->ports/volumes, yet they are freed (cli_main.c:70-71), passed (93-96), validated (start.c:814-815), copied (start.c:1357-1362), serialized behind count>0 guards that are never true (record.c:282-289), reloaded (record.c:754-758 via the json.c:436-442 wrappers), and replayed on redial (call.c:146-148). Reading them back only serves records from deleted pre-cut versions — a legacy shim against the pinned workstyle.
plays.risks: A symbol could be reached via conditional compilation or a macro the greps missed; the reload deletion means a record hand-written (or written by a pre-cut binary) with ports/volumes keys silently loses them on redial. Mitigation: nm cross-reference over all objects before deleting, then zero-warning build + 135/135 tests + layer lint, all confirmed green at baseline this session.
assumptions: No out-of-tree consumer links against libhold objects (single static binary, no installed headers — consistent with the identity doc).

The record.c crash-leftover-tmp fix (RiskLedger, record.c:163) will NOT be implemented via hold_open_unique_temp; if the owner prefers that remedy, keep that one 26-line function and delete the other six.
Keeping the -p/-v honest rejections and the separate observed_ports projection untouched — they are contract-pinned (docker-parity-contract honesty clause, test_040_dockerish.sh).
plays.strongest_objection: Dead-code deletion buys zero user-visible improvement, and some of these functions (hold_open_unique_temp, hold_fsync_dir_path) look like exactly the primitives the ledger's store-durability fixes will want next week — deleting then re-adding is churn. Answer: the repo's git history preserves them, the <10k direction is a stated contract, and a declaration with no definition (hold_sha256_file_hex) is a live link trap today, not hypothetical debt.
plays.unique_insight: The ports/volumes plumbing is not merely dead — it is *unreachable-by-construction* dead: the only writers were deleted with the -p/-v rejection, so the count>0 serialization guards in record.c:282-289 are provably never true, which means deletion is behavior-identical for every record the current binary can produce. That makes this the rare deletion that needs no behavioral test change at all.
plays.change_sketch: Pass 1 (pure dead code): delete layout.c:10-11,13-21,161-263 and store.h:8,14-18; delete the seven dead export definitions and their header declarations. Pass 2 (write-never plumbing): remove portc/ports/volumec/volumes from struct run_options (cli_main.c:54-56) and the recipe structs in types.h:16-19 / runtime.h:24-27; drop the free/pass-through sites (cli_main.c:70-71,93-96), validation (start.c:798-800,814-815), copy (start.c:1357-1362), frees (start.c:1513-1524), serialization (record.c:282-289), reload (record.c:754-761), redial replay (call.c:146-148), and the two json.c wrappers (436-442) with core.h:58-59. Verify: nm -o obj/**/*.o cross-ref before each deletion, then make (zero warnings), make test (135/135), bash scripts/lint_layers.sh, wc -l src to record the line-count delta (~320 lines).

plays.thesis: Make plain log output behave like cat: delete the legacy JSON-log decode shim (hold_decode_json_log_line) and the entire decoded_log_stream buffering layer built on it, so every read path emits the raw captured bytes SPEC.md promises — fixing the confirmed corruption where a user line that happens to look like {"log":...} is silently rewritten in logs -p, tail, and the viewer.
plays.improves: Fidelity first: this converts the ledger's high-severity FACT (filter.c:6/204 finding — logs -p prints 'SNEAKY-decoded' instead of the literal JSON line on disk) into a doc-sanctioned fix. SPEC.md's Logs section (read this session) pins 'Raw captured bytes plus the HLOGIDX sidecar' and identity.md:63 pins '-p: plain dump, always script-safe'; test_hold.sh:874 already asserts no writer produces the JSON wrapper format, so the decoder can only ever fire on user data, i.e. it is pure corruption. Deletion removes: hold_decode_json_log_line (logging.c:389-412), the dead hold_write_json_log_entry_fd/bytes_fd wrappers (logging.c:381-387) and core.h:119-121 decls, the ~58-line decoded_log_stream layer in signal.c:29-87 (tail then writes read bytes straight through hold_write_all — simpler and drops a per-chunk realloc/malloc), and the decode detours in filter.c consume_line (201-207, 210, 214, 220, 223) and the backward scanner (434-440, 447) — which also removes a malloc+strdup per visible line from the viewer hot path. Filtering then matches what is actually on disk.
plays.risks: Behavior change on log display: anyone holding pre-0.5-era logs written in the old JSON format would now see raw JSON lines instead of decoded text. That is the definition of a legacy shim the owner's workstyle forbids, and the shim's cost (corrupting current, correct data) outstrips its benefit (prettifying data no supported version writes). Mechanical risk is low: the three call sites are isolated, and the new regression test pins the fix.
assumptions: No compatibility promise exists for logs written by pre-0.5 binaries (no normative doc read this session makes one; SPEC describes only the raw+HLOGIDX pair).
The tail path can pass raw read() chunks directly to stdout without line reassembly once decoding is gone (the buffering existed only to feed whole lines to the decoder).
dup_line/push_visible in filter.c work unchanged when 'visible' always aliases the raw buffer.
plays.strongest_objection: This is a behavior change on three user-visible read paths landed as a 'deletion', and the viewer's similar/filter matching will now see raw JSON text for any legacy log, changing match results for those users. Answer: the normative docs say the current behavior is wrong (SPEC raw-bytes clause, identity -p script-safe clause), the ledger verified live corruption of current-format data, and the change is pinned by a new round-trip test — this is exactly the 'behavior preserved unless a normative doc says current behavior is wrong' anchor exception.
plays.unique_insight: The write side of this format was already deleted — hold_write_json_log_entry_fd/bytes_fd are dead wrappers that just forward to the raw writer, and test_hold.sh:874 asserts the wrapper format never appears in logs. So the codebase currently maintains a decoder for a format it also asserts can never exist: the two halves of the code contradict each other, and only the deletion makes them consistent.
plays.change_sketch: Delete logging.c:381-412 (two dead write wrappers + hold_decode_json_log_line) and core.h:119-121. In signal.c, delete struct decoded_log_stream and its four helpers (29-87) and rewrite hold_tail_log_until_exit's inner loop (192-234) to hold_write_all(STDOUT_FILENO, buf, n) directly. In filter.c, strip the decode locals from consume_line (visible=line, visible_n=n stay) and from the backward scanner block at 434-447. Add one regression test near the existing log-capture test (test_hold.sh ~866): launch sh -c 'echo {"log":"SNEAKY","stream":"stdout"}', assert logs -p emits the line byte-identical (grep -F for the full JSON text), register it in the test list. Verify: make, make test (136/136), plus a manual isolated-HOME probe of logs -p / -f / __view on the fixture.

plays.thesis: Collapse the three copies of the /proc/<pid>/stat pgid/sid/state parser onto the one strict implementation in platform/process.c, exporting it through platform.h — deleting the two runtime copies (observe.c, shell.c) whose bare strtol parsing silently accepts malformed tokens that feed group-membership decisions on the signal/observe path.
plays.improves: Fidelity and consistency of the control plane. Verified this session: read_process_ids_state (process.c:42-108) validates every token via parse_pid_token (end/errno/positivity checks, process.c:29-38), while hold_proc_read_ids (observe.c:45-97) and shell_read_proc_ids (shell.c:132-180) are byte-for-byte the same open/EINTR-read/strrchr(')')/strtok_r skeleton but parse with bare strtol(tok, NULL, 10) — no error detection at observe.c:81/84 and shell.c:164/167. Those two lax copies feed stats/ports group membership (observe.c:232) and the guarded-shell pgid adoption walk (shell.c:194). One exported reader gives a single validation policy — fail closed on malformed stat lines — and deletes ~100 duplicated lines. Layer-safe by the repo's own lint (runtime may include hold/platform.h; shell.c:6 already does; scripts/lint_layers.sh read and run clean this session).
plays.risks: Behavior delta at the margins: the strict parser returns -1 where the lax copies fabricated a pid from a malformed token — strictly an improvement, but if any test implicitly relied on lax acceptance it would surface. The __APPLE__ story must be preserved: observe.c:423-427 currently provides an ENOSYS stub for non-Linux; the platform export needs the same guard so the macOS build stays clean.
assumptions: No caller of hold_proc_read_ids exists outside observe.c:232 (grep this session: definition, ENOSYS stub, one call site, observe.h:22 decl — nothing else).
Exposing the parser as a platform export (e.g. hold_read_proc_group_ids) with the existing hold_read_proc_stat_tokens naming convention is acceptable API shape for platform.h.
The group-kill, stats, ports, and shell tests in test_hold.sh exercise these paths enough to catch a wiring mistake.
plays.strongest_objection: This is consolidation for its own sake — no user has hit the lax-parse divergence, and touching the signal-adjacent scan path risks more than it saves. Answer: the ledger's Safety Red-teamer flagged exactly this divergence as the mechanism by which a future parser fix lands in one copy and not the others, and the signal path's SPEC safety invariant makes 'four parsers, three policies' the kind of quiet drift that eventually becomes a wrong-group kill; the change is mechanical, ~100 lines net negative, and fully covered by the existing suite.
plays.unique_insight: The duplication is not stylistic — it is a validation-policy fork on the safety-critical path: the one place the strict parser lives (platform) is used by liveness checks for signaling, while the two lax copies serve observe/adopt decisions about the same groups. Consolidating is therefore a hardening change disguised as a cleanup: it upgrades two decision sites to the validation discipline the SPEC invariant already assumes they have. Optional same-session extension: fold process.c's own second parser (hold_read_proc_stat_tokens' Linux branch, 269-318) onto the same static token-walk helper, finishing the ledger's four-parsers-to-one arc.
plays.change_sketch: In process.c, drop the static qualifier from read_process_ids_state (rename to hold_read_proc_group_ids), add the #else __APPLE__ ENOSYS branch (moved from observe.c:423-427), declare it in platform.h next to hold_read_proc_stat_tokens (platform.h:19). Delete observe.c:45-97 and the 423-427 stub, retarget observe.c:232 to the new export, add #include "hold/platform.h" to observe.c, delete observe.h:22. Delete shell_read_proc_ids (shell.c:132-180) and retarget shell.c:194 (platform.h already included at shell.c:6). Verify: make (zero warnings), make test (135/135 — group-kill, stats, ports, and shell tests cover all retargeted call sites), bash scripts/lint_layers.sh clean.

plays.thesis: Signal-target validation must fail closed: an identity token that exists but was never compared authorizes nothing, an unreadable /proc/<pid>/exe is 'unverified' not 'verified', and SPEC.md's absolute 'a recycled PID is never signaled' is narrowed to the guarantee the code can actually keep — with the leader-gone group fallback additionally refusing any surviving member that started before the recorded leader.
plays.improves: Closes three ledger findings at once: signal.c:150 (leader-gone fallback authorizes kill(-pgid) on bare (pgid,sid) match, checking only that r->proc_starttime_ticks or exe_dev/ino are NONZERO in the record, never comparing them to any live process — I read validate_signal_target lines 112-157 this session), signal.c:121-129 (exe revalidation rejects only when hold_read_proc_exe returns 0 AND mismatches; a -1 return silently passes, then line 138's have_identity_token check converts verification failure into success), and signal.c:263/298 TOCTOU vs SPEC.md:59-60's overclaim. Concrete attack closed: a stale 'running' record whose (pgid,sid) now matches an unrelated pre-existing same-user group — any member older than the recorded leader start can never be the call's descendant, so hold stop refuses (rc 2, the existing 'identity differs' message) instead of delivering TERM-then-KILL to a foreign group. Test that proves it: new shell test beside test_signal_refuses_tampered_live_group_identity (tests/test_hold.sh:750) that forges a record pointing pid at a reaped PID and pgid/sid at a live decoy call's group with proc_starttime_ticks set above the decoy's start, then asserts hold stop exits 2 and the decoy's sleep is still alive; second test uses an #ifdef HOLD_TESTING env hook (HOLD_TEST_FAIL_PROC_EXE=1, matching the repo's existing guard discipline at store/layout.c:111) to force exe-read failure on a starttime-less record and asserts refusal rather than kill.
plays.risks: The starttime-floor check needs the same tick units on both sides (Linux boot-relative ticks; macOS microsecond wallclock per process.c:260-265 — both self-consistent within a boot, and boot id is already checked at signal.c:107). Adopted 'hold on' groups could theoretically contain a member that joined via setpgid from an older process; pipeline members all start at/after the group leader, so this is edge-rare, but a false refusal on a live adopted call is the failure mode to watch in test runs.
assumptions: hold_group_session_liveness (platform/process.c:111-190) is the right place to grow a starttime-aware member scan; read_process_ids_state already validates tokens so the new helper inherits the strict parser
No existing test depends on the leader-gone GROUP_LIVE fallback succeeding with an uncompared token (test_hold.sh:750 covers only the live-leader tamper case per the ledger)
Owner accepts one new HOLD_TESTING-guarded fault hook; the repo already has the pattern and the ledger separately faults the two UNguarded ones
plays.strongest_objection: The starttime floor does NOT close the marquee pid-wrap attack in the ledger: a recycled (pgid,sid) implies the counter wrapped, so the foreign members' starttimes are LATER than the recorded leader's and the floor check passes. The genuine closure for that case is the SPEC.md:59-60 rewording ('never knowingly signaled; identity revalidated immediately before every delivery'), and a skeptic can fairly say the hardening half only closes the accidental-collision case while the docs half closes the adversarial one — hardening and honesty must land together or the play is cosmetic.
plays.unique_insight: The code's own error string at signal.c:139 — 'has no recorded process identity token' — reveals the design bug: possession of a token was treated as proof of identity. Every refusal path compares; the two authorize paths (line 142 and 156) only check presence. Making presence-without-comparison a refusal is a three-line inversion that the existing error messages already narrate correctly.
plays.change_sketch: (1) signal.c:124: treat hold_read_proc_exe(...) != 0 as identity-not-confirmed when starttime==0 and exe is the only token: refuse rc 2 with a new 'identity unverifiable' message, mirroring the strict starttime handling at 117-119. (2) Add hold_group_min_member_starttime(pgid, sid, uint64_t *min_out) to platform/process.c reusing the validated read_process_ids_state + hold_read_proc_stat_tokens walk; in the gl==GROUP_LIVE fallback (signal.c:151-157), when r->proc_starttime_ticks != 0, refuse if any live member's starttime < recorded leader starttime. (3) As the last act before each kill (signal.c:263 and 298), re-run the leader stat token read once more (cheap shrink of the TOCTOU window). (4) Reword SPEC.md:59-60 from the absolute claim to 'never knowingly signaled; the recorded identity is revalidated immediately before every delivery'. (5) Two shell tests as described; register them in the suite table. Build stays C11 zero-warning; no new deps.

plays.thesis: A crash mid-write must never brick a call's record, and the purge sweep must respect the store's own two-phase creation protocol: switch record/public-index writers to the already-shipped-but-dead hold_open_unique_temp, make the orphan sweeps honor the .reserve marker they currently ignore, collect the .log.idx and .tmp litter they currently leak, and report corrupt-record removals instead of deleting silently.
plays.improves: Closes four ledger findings with one coherent store-lifecycle change set, all in code I read this session. (a) record.c:163 opens a FIXED-name '.%s.tmp' with O_EXCL and unlinks it only on its own failure (record.c:384-386), so a SIGKILL mid-write leaves a tomb that makes every future rewrite of that call fail 'File exists' — rename dies fatally and the reaper's mark-finished silently exhausts (verified live in the ledger). fs.c:60-85 already contains hold_open_unique_temp (pid+random nonce in the name, declared at core.h:28, zero callers — confirmed by grep this session): using it in hold_write_record_atomic and hold_write_public_index_atomic (record.c:401 has the same fixed-name pattern) removes the EEXIST failure mode entirely while reviving a dead export the minimalism sweep would otherwise delete. (b) list.c:748-752 and :784-786 unlink any <id>.log/<id>.sock whose runs/<id>.json is absent — but start.c holds .<id>.reserve for exactly the window where log and socket exist before the JSON (start.c:915-927, read this session), and layout.c:169's id_collides_in_store already treats the reserve as 'id taken'; the sweep just forgot the same check, so a concurrent purge deletes a nascent call's live log and console socket. (c) list.c:700-703 unlinks r.log_path without unlink_log_index_for_log, orphaning <id>.log.idx forever (the targeted path at 640-643 gets it right — the fix is one call plus a *.log.idx orphan pass in sweep_logs). (d) list.c:681-688 unlinks unloadable/invalid records with no stderr and no stats, violating SPEC.md's 'corrupt records are reported' — add the same 'skipping corrupt record' warning list.c uses and count it, so a corrupt-only sweep no longer prints 'nothing to purge' after deleting files. Attack/failure closed: kill -9 during hold rename → call permanently un-rewritable; hold purge racing hold -d → live log capture into an unlinked inode and a dead console socket. Tests: plant .<id>.tmp then assert rename and mark-finished still succeed; plant .<id>.reserve + <id>.log with no JSON, run purge -a, assert the log survives; full lifecycle then purge, assert zero *.log.idx remain; plant a truncated record, purge, assert stderr warning and non-'nothing to purge' summary.
plays.risks: Unique temp names remove the accidental mutual exclusion the fixed O_EXCL name provided: two concurrent writers of the same record now both succeed via rename, last-write-wins silently. The purge summary wording changes when corrupt records are swept, so any test grepping the exact 'nothing to purge' string in a corrupt-fixture scenario needs checking (I found the two message sites at list.c:866/962; existing tests don't plant corrupt records before a sweep per the ledger).
assumptions: No external tooling depends on the transient '.<id>.tmp' name shape (it lives <1ms in normal operation and the store dirs are 0700)

The .reserve marker is reliably unlinked on every start failure path (start.c:905-1050 shows unlink_if_nonempty on each exit; a crashed start leaves a stale reserve, so the sweep should skip-but-count reserves whose embedded creation is old rather than skip forever — sketch handles this by also sweeping .reserve/.tmp litter whose writer pid is dead)
hold_open_unique_temp's 0600/0644 mode parameter reproduces the exact permission behavior of the current open calls including the fchmod/fchown at record.c:405-410
plays.strongest_objection: Switching to unique temps makes the unserialized read-modify-write race (record.c:1015, rename/save vs reaper mark-finished — a separate ledger finding) strictly easier to hit silently: today the second overlapping writer at least fails loudly on EEXIST; after this change it clobbers quietly. The honest answer is that today's behavior is not protection (the loser's update is lost either way, plus the call bricks), but the real fix for lost updates is a per-id flock, which this play consciously defers — a skeptic can call that closing one hole while widening the announcement of another.
plays.unique_insight: The store already contains both halves of its own fix and applies each in only one place: fs.c ships the crash-safe unique-temp primitive that record.c hand-rolled badly, and layout.c:169 already encodes 'a .reserve means this id is mid-birth' — start.c's collision check consults it but the purge sweep, the only other reader who needed it, never does. This play is not new machinery; it is making the store's existing invariants apply uniformly.
plays.change_sketch: (1) record.c hold_write_record_atomic: replace the fixed-name open at :163 with hold_open_unique_temp(dir, r->id, 0600, tmp, sizeof(tmp)); same in hold_write_public_index_atomic at :401 with mode 0644; the existing rename-to-final and failure-unlink paths (:384-386, :463) are unchanged. (2) list.c sweep_logs/sweep_consoles: before unlinking an orphan, snprintf the '.<id>.reserve' path and skip when hold_path_exists (mirrors layout.c:169 and start.c:155). (3) list.c:700: add unlink_log_index_for_log(r.log_path) beside the log unlink; extend sweep_logs to also collect '<id>.log.idx' files whose record JSON is absent (same id-extraction pattern already used at :731-740). (4) list.c:681-688: fprintf(stderr, "hold: warning: removing corrupt record %s\n", e->d_name) and stats->removed++ on both unlink branches. (5) Optional litter pass: collect dot-prefixed *.tmp/*.reserve entries whose embedded writer pid (unique-temp names carry it) no longer exists. (6) Four shell tests as listed, registered in the suite table.

plays.source: capability-security (distant domain: authority derived from structure, not from stored claims)
plays.thesis: Purge must derive its deletion targets from (store layout, call id) instead of obeying path strings stored inside the record: hold_prune_one_run and the sweep currently unlink whatever log_path/console_sock a record file claims (record.c:624-628 and :677-680 accept them verbatim — console_sock's only check is a leading '/'), which means the one destructive verb executes arbitrary unlinks on behalf of a corrupt or hostile record — worst on the root-run system store.
plays.improves: Closes the validate.c:82 ledger finding (hold_valid_record checks only pid>0, pgid>1, id shape — I read validate.c:82-85 and the load paths this session) by making the confinement check unnecessary rather than adding one: every writer constructs log_path as store->log_dir/<id>.log and console_sock as store->console_dir/<id>.sock, so purge recomputing those exact paths from the store it already holds (hold_prune_one_run takes const struct hold_store *, list.c:626; the sweep loop has it too) is behavior-identical for every record hold ever wrote, while a record whose stored paths point elsewhere loses all power — the foreign path is simply never touched. Concrete attack closed: root runs 'hold purge -s'; a (root-written but corrupt, or tampered-on-disk) record in the system store carries "log_path": "/etc/shadow" or console_sock aimed at another service's socket; today list.c:640-646 / :700-706 unlink it as root. After this play the unlink targets are computed, the record is additionally reported as suspect when its stored paths disagree with the computed ones, and /etc/shadow survives. Test that proves it closed: shell test plants a well-formed record whose log_path points at a victim file inside HOME (fixture pattern already exists — the suite writes fake records/public entries around test_hold.sh:2492), runs purge --force on it, asserts rc 0, the victim file still exists, the store-resident <id>.log/<id>.log.idx are gone, and stderr carries the path-mismatch warning. SPEC.md:62's 'records are trusted only after validation' finally has teeth on the destructive path.
plays.risks: Low: unlink of a computed path that does not exist is a harmless ENOENT (the current code already relies on that for has_console). The behavioral delta only appears for records whose stored paths diverge from the layout — which no current writer produces. Redial's append path (call.c:115-119 per the ledger) still honors the stored log_path; this play scopes to the destructive verb and notes redial as the follow-up, so the trust inversion is deliberately partial.
assumptions: No supported flow lets a record's log legitimately live outside store->log_dir (grep this session found log_path constructed only by start.c from the store layout; no --log-path flag exists in cli_main.c's flag tables)
Sidecar path derivation (hold_log_idx_path, used at list.c:621) composes correctly from the computed log path exactly as it does from the stored one
Existing 135 tests never plant a record with divergent paths and expect purge to follow them (ledger's own probes plus the fixtures I read suggest none do)
plays.strongest_objection: Ignoring the record's path fields hard-codes the assumption that logs are forever store-resident; if a future feature adds user-specified log locations, purge will silently stop cleaning them and the leak will look like a regression with no error. Also, after a store relocation (HOME change, migration), records whose stored paths point at the old location would previously have been cleaned by following the record — under derivation the old files are stranded. Both are answered by the mismatch warning (the stranding is at least announced), but a skeptic can fairly say this play trades a hypothetical root-unlink attack for a real, if minor, cleanup gap in migration scenarios.
plays.unique_insight: Purge already possesses every byte it needs — the store struct and the validated 64-hex id — to name its deletion targets; the record's log_path/console_sock fields carry zero information the layout doesn't, so on the destructive path they are pure attack surface. The strongest validation of untrusted input is to stop consuming it: deletion-by-derivation removes the vulnerability class (path confinement bypasses, symlink games in stored strings, prefix-check bugs) instead of guarding it, and it is a net code subtraction in the two unlink sites.
plays.change_sketch: (1) Add a static helper in list.c: compute_call_paths(store, id, log, sock) via hold_checked_snprintf of '%s/%s.log' and '%s/%s.sock'. (2) hold_prune_one_run (list.c:626-653): replace unlink(r.log_path)/unlink(r.console_sock) with unlinks of the computed paths (idx via unlink_log_index_for_log on the computed log); when r.has_log and strcmp(r.log_path, computed) != 0, fprintf(stderr, "hold: warning: record %s names a log outside the store; left untouched: %s\n", ...) — same for console_sock. (3) Same substitution in the sweep branch (list.c:700-706). (4) One sentence in SPEC.md pinning 'purge removes only store-resident artifacts derived from the call id; paths stored in records are never followed for deletion'. (5) The planted-victim shell test described above, registered in the suite table. Pure C11, no new deps, net negative lines in list.c.

plays.posture: First-principles (Contract Warden): if the bare launch form were designed fresh, every flag would be either in the parity contract's closed table or honestly rejected like -p/-v. Nothing silently remapped, nothing undocumented-but-live.
plays.thesis: Close the launch-flag surface: execute the identity doc's '--cap paths: delete' cut, delete the --privileged silent remap, and amend docker-parity-contract.md so the flag table plus an honest-rejection list is exhaustive and test-pinned.
plays.improves: Removes the worst muscle-memory trap in intake: --privileged (parsed at src/cli_main.c:136-139, remapped to requested_system at 342-343) silently reinterprets Docker's capability-granting flag as store scoping, then fails non-root with a message that never names the flag typed. Removes ~200 lines of raw SYS_capget/SYS_capset machinery (--cap-add/--cap-drop parsing at cli_main.c:397-428, apply_child_capabilities in start.c, recipe plumbing in call.c/record.c) that the superior contract (hold-on-identity.md:187) orders deleted and the subordinate contract's closed flag list (docker-parity-contract.md:16-27) does not license. Also documents or folds --restart-delay (cli_main.c:461-478), the third doc-absent flag. Converts five ledger findings (two high 'privileged', two high/medium 'cap', one restart-delay) in one amendment.
plays.risks: Deleting working, test-pinned functionality: tests/test_hold.sh:2521-2574 assert real CapEff changes and CapAdd/CapDrop recipe round-trip, and those three tests must be removed with the code. The Docker-shaped record contract test at test_hold.sh:2960 and the fixture at 3024 pin Config.CapAdd/CapDrop/Privileged keys — mitigated by keeping the record shape (emit empty arrays; Privileged is already uid-derived at src/store/record.c:143, untouched by the flag deletion), so only the three behavior tests go.
assumptions: hold-on-identity.md is the superior normative doc despite its draft banner (docker-parity-contract.md:5 declares itself subordinate; SPEC.md defers to it), so its cut table sanctions the behavior change.
No user depends on --privileged's remap: it is documented nowhere current, has zero tests, and only appears in docs/archive/.

Keeping Config.CapAdd/CapDrop as always-empty keys satisfies the 2960/3024 Docker-shape pins without a legacy read shim (loader keeps reading them only as inert record fields or the keys are dropped from both test and writer together in the same commit).
plays.strongest_objection: --cap-drop ALL demonstrably works for non-root users and is genuinely useful for sandboxing long-held services; deleting shipped, test-pinned capability on the authority of a doc still stamped 'draft for redline' may be exactly backwards — the owner might want the opposite amendment (add both flags to the parity table and help). The play only wins if the owner ratifies the cut; the change sketch therefore lands the contract amendment text first so reversing the decision is a one-paragraph edit, not an archaeology dig.
plays.unique_insight: The --privileged and --cap-* problems are one problem: the parity contract says 'Tests assert the shapes written here' and presents a closed flag list, but parse_run_options is an open set — three flags live outside the contract, one of them actively lying about its Docker meaning. Closing the set (delete + honest rejection paragraph naming --privileged/--cap-*/-p/-v together) makes the contract self-enforcing: any future flag must either enter the table with tests or be rejected with a message, because a rejection test will catch the fallthrough.
plays.change_sketch: 1) docker-parity-contract.md: extend the honest-rejection paragraph (currently -p/-P/-v at lines 32-34) to name --privileged and --cap-add/--cap-drop ('capabilities are a container-substrate concept; run under a user with the rights you need'), and add --restart-delay to the flag table since it is already tested. 2) cli_main.c: delete the --privileged branch in parse_docker_run_flag (136-139) and the remap (342-343); replace --cap-add/--cap-drop parsing (397-428) with reject_caps_option() mirroring reject_publish_option (282-286), exit 5. 3) start.c: delete apply_child_capabilities and its call sites plus the cap fields threading in hold_perform_start_options (visible at start.c:803-806); call.c: drop cap replay; record.c: emit empty CapAdd/CapDrop arrays, delete the load/round-trip of cap fields; types.h/runtime.h: drop fields. 4) tests: delete the three cap-behavior tests (test_hold.sh:2521-2574 region, registrations near 4213-4215); add rejection-shape tests for --privileged and --cap-add next to the -p/-v rejection tests; keep 2960/3024 green via the empty-array emission. 5) hold-on-identity.md: mark the line-187 cut row executed. Verify: make (zero warnings), full test suite, plus manual probe that `hold --privileged /bin/true` and `hold --cap-drop ALL true` now print the honest stderr message and exit 5.

plays.posture: First-principles (Contract Warden): rebuilt from the outcome backward, an error path has exactly two channels — a one-line diagnostic on stderr and a nonzero exit; stdout is reserved for machine data. And the help screen is a contract surface: it may not describe a verb relationship the code deliberately abolished.
plays.thesis: Make usage errors obey the pinned output discipline (stderr on nonzero exit) and make the help/usage text stop contradicting the ps/list divergence contract — six ledger findings, one small change set, all pinned with new tests.
plays.improves: Fidelity of the scripting contract docker-parity-contract.md:66-69 declares normative: today `hold -d` with an empty command prints the full 1.7KB help to stdout and exits 5 (cli_main.c:549-552), so id=$(hold -d "$CMD") captures a banner as a call id; the unknown-command fallthrough does the same at rc 1 (cli_main.c:930-932); hold_perform_start_options prints hold_usage() to stdout on invalid options (start.c:777-780 and the arg-validation branch ending at 813-820). Truthfulness of the primary help surface: commands.c:79 and README.md ('the call table (ps is an alias)') teach the pre-0.6 model that docker-parity-contract.md:38-39, the dispatch comment at cli_main.c:712-714, and the rc=1 ps-scope-flag tests all contradict. Plus the broken promise at commands.c:94: `hold list -h` / `hold ps -h` currently die 'unknown flag' rc 1 because the shared owned-flag loop rejects dashes before any -h case (cli_main.c:633-639), and the purge spec usage at cli.c:42 omits the -s/-u flags its own help topic (cli.c:147) documents.
plays.risks: Low. The deliberate stdout help paths stay untouched: bare `hold` rc 0 (cli_main.c:499-503, pinned by the 'PLACE A CALL' stdout test at tests/test_hold.sh:815-819) and --help/-h rc 0 (cli_main.c:652-655). Any script parsing the old stdout-help-on-error behavior breaks — but that behavior is precisely what the normative contract calls wrong, so this is a sanctioned fix, not a silent divergence.
assumptions: Exit codes stay as-is (5 for the flags-only path, 1 for unknown-command fallthrough); reconciling the rc-5-vs-documented-1 split in help_scripting (cli.c:100-107) is a separate contract decision deliberately left out of scope.
No test pins the 'ps is an alias' string or stdout-on-error (verified: the only bare-hold pin greps 'PLACE A CALL' on the rc-0 path).
Adding -h handling to list/ps is contract-fulfilling, not surface growth, because commands.c:94 already promises 'hold <command> -h  help for one command' for every verb.
plays.strongest_objection: This is a grab-bag of small fixes rather than one idea, and it knowingly leaves the exit-code-table contradiction (usage errors documented as 1 but returned as 5, both pinned by tests) unresolved — a Warden play that fixes the channel but not the code number is only half the discipline. Answer: channel and phrasing fixes are zero-contention and land in a session; the 1-vs-5 unification requires an owner ruling that would touch two existing test pins, so it is staged, not skipped.
plays.unique_insight: Every one of these six defects is the same defect: hold_usage() is treated as a universal fallback renderer, so it leaks onto the wrong stream, at the wrong exit codes, carrying a stale sentence. Constraining it to exactly two call sites (bare hold, explicit --help/-h) and giving every error path a one-line stderr usage instead makes the output discipline structural — the contract stops depending on each branch author remembering which stream to use.
plays.change_sketch: 1) cli_main.c:549-552: replace hold_usage() with fprintf(stderr, "usage: hold [flags] <cmd|id|name> [args...]\n") keeping rc 5; same one-liner at the fallthrough 930-932 (rc 1). 2) start.c: the two hold_usage() calls in hold_perform_start_options' validation paths become the same stderr one-liner. 3) commands.c:79: split into 'hold list — your scoped ledger (live and past)' and a new line 'hold ps — Docker's machine-wide running view'; fix the matching README sentence. 4) cli_main.c owned-flag loop: before the unknown-flag rejection at 633, if arg is -h/--help route to hold_show_help(command) rc 0 (covers list/ps; other verbs already handled at 669-673). 5) cli.c:42-44: add [-s|--system] [-u|--user] to the purge/rm/prune/drop usage strings, matching help_action at 147. 6) Tests: assert `hold -d` exits nonzero with empty stdout and a stderr usage line; `hold list -h` rc 0 mentioning 'scoped ledger'; `hold ps -h` rc 0; keep 815-819 green. Verify with make + full suite.

plays.posture: First-principles (Contract Warden): a promise of 'a documented, stable format' is only a contract if the bytes are written down somewhere normative and a test fails when they drift. Today the only written layout actively mis-decodes the shipped bits, and three 'current' reference docs describe a deleted product.
plays.thesis: Make SPEC.md's HLOGIDX promise true: write the shipped v1 byte layout into a rewritten docs/store.md as the normative format, pin it with a byte-level test plus the two untested sidecar-recovery rules, and archive the pre-0.6 reference docs (launcher.md, console.md, old store.md) that index.md still lists as current.
plays.improves: SPEC.md:50-54 and hold-on-identity.md:29-31 promise the .log/.log.idx pair is 'a documented, stable format so anything else can read Hold's logs without Hold's help' — but no current doc gives the layout, and the only written spec (docs/future/viewer-fixes-before-0.5.md:413-422, 3-bit stream field, NO_NEWLINE=0x0008) contradicts the shipped writer (src/core/logging.c:13-16: STDERR=0x0001, NO_NEWLINE=0x0002, CONTINUATION=0x0004, TRUNCATED=0x0008), so an external reader following the doc decodes every stderr partial line as STDIN. Meanwhile docs/index.md:29-32 presents launcher.md/store.md/console.md as current though they document profiles.json/aliases.json, 'hold tail/start/dump', 12-hex IDs, and self-elevation, with nav links to files that exist only under docs/archive/. Also closes the Test Auditor gap: the crash-recovery min(header,physical) rule (logging.c:265-271) and the 1MiB TRUNCATED clamp have zero coverage.
plays.risks: Documenting v1 freezes bits that other ledger findings show are cramped: the single STDERR bit cannot represent STDIN/PTY streams (console captures are written as stdout), so a future console-labeling fix needs a version bump. Mitigation: the header already carries a version field (HOLD_LOGIDX_VERSION 1, logging.c:6); the doc states v1 explicitly and defines reader behavior on unknown versions, which is what makes a clean v2 possible rather than impossible. Doc-only + test-only change, so the 135-test baseline is structurally safe.
assumptions: The shipped writer is the intended v1 (the format is already on disk in every user's store since 0.5/0.6, so the code, not the future-doc proposal, is the truth to pin).

Moving launcher.md/console.md to docs/archive/ and rewriting store.md is sanctioned by index.md's own rule that 'documentation for removed subsystems lives in archive/' — no normative invariant lives only in those files (SPEC.md carries the invariants; anything current found there gets folded into the rewrite before archiving).
A byte-level shell test can parse the sidecar portably with the python3 already used by existing tests (e.g. the CapEff and record-shape checks in tests/test_hold.sh).
plays.strongest_objection: This play writes prose and tests but fixes none of the live viewer bugs the ledger ranks higher (first-50 truncation, tail-N inversion, stale-sidecar acceptance) — a skeptic will say pinning the byte format is polishing the contract while the product misreads its own logs. Answer: those are Runtime Mechanic plays on the same area; the Warden's job is that the format promise is currently unfalsifiable and the only written spec is actively wrong for third parties — and the stale-sidecar fix (raw_size/raw_mtime validation) cannot even be specified until the header fields' meaning is written down somewhere normative.
plays.unique_insight: The repo's contract discipline ('contracts pinned in docs + tests') has a blind spot for on-disk formats: record JSON is pinned by shape tests, CLI shapes by shell tests, but the one format explicitly marketed as consumable without Hold's help is pinned by neither doc nor test — and the raw_size_bytes/raw_mtime_ns staleness fields written on every append (logging.c:340-341) are meaningless to third parties until documented. Writing the layout down is not documentation housekeeping; it is the missing half of the store's contract, and the byte-level test makes 'stable' mean something mechanical.
plays.change_sketch: 1) Rewrite docs/store.md for 0.6: current layout (runs/, logs with <id>.log + <id>.log.idx, console/, public/), drop profiles.json/aliases.json/requires_elevation and the dead header links; add a normative 'HLOGIDX v1' section transcribed from src/core/logging.c:4-19 — magic "HLOGIDX\0", version 1, 80-byte little-endian header (base_unix_us, entry_count, raw_size_bytes, raw_mtime_ns), 16-byte entries packing 44-bit offset + 20-bit length and 48-bit delta-us + 16-bit meta, meta bits STDERR=0x0001/NO_NEWLINE=0x0002/CONTINUATION=0x0004/TRUNCATED=0x0008, the ascending-offset invariant, and the min(header,physical) crash-recovery rule. 2) Stamp docs/future/viewer-fixes-before-0.5.md's layout block 'superseded by docs/store.md (shipped v1 differs: single-bit stream)'. 3) git mv launcher.md and console.md to docs/archive/, update index.md:29-32 and the SPEC.md reference lines. 4) Tests: shell test launches `hold -d sh -c 'echo out; echo err 1>&2'`, then python3 asserts magic/version/header size and that the stderr entry's meta has exactly bit 0x0001 set at the documented offsets; viewer_filter_test.c gains the truncated-idx and inflated-header-count recovery cases against build_indexed_log. Verify: make, full suite, link-check the docs index by grep.

plays.source: telephony-metaphor (PBX attendant transfer: the operator patches the call through and gets off the line)
plays.thesis: Anchor console-call records to the held process group instead of the broker by reusing the existing target_pid_pipe handshake, so 'hold end' hangs up on the party, not the switchboard operator — closing the #1 critical finding where a SIGHUP-ignoring target escapes while hold fabricates 'Exited (0)'.
plays.improves: Signal fidelity and exit-status honesty for every console call. Today start.c:1293-1295 records r.pid=r.pgid=r.sid = the broker's pid, so signal.c:263 kill(-r.pgid, SIGTERM) kills the broker outright: broker_cleanup_and_exit and hold_mark_run_finished never run, the socket leaks on disk, and the target keeps running unmanaged. After the fix, the record points at the real call group (the target does setsid+TIOCSCTTY at broker.c:205-216, so pid==pgid==sid holds for it exactly as the record shape expects), SPEC.md's 'signals delivered to the revalidated call group' becomes true, identity tokens at start.c:1389-1390 (starttime, exe dev/ino) are read from the actual target, and the broker's existing WNOHANG reap + post-EOF waitpid (broker.c:311-321, 421-437) records the true exit status and unlinks the socket on its normal path.
plays.risks: The broker now outlives 'hold end' by up to one poll cycle while it drains the PTY and marks the record finished, so 'end then immediately list' can briefly show running — tests must poll record state (the suite already has record_ended_soon helpers). The new pipe write in the broker must happen after exec-handshake success (broker.c:247-251) and the write end must be closed on every broker_fail_errno path or the parent's read at start.c:1241 blocks. Adopted-console path (hold_run_console_broker_adopted) is untouched but shares broker_serve, so the new parameter must default to -1 there.
assumptions: The parent-side target_pid_pipe reader at start.c:1241-1261 (which reassigns pid and rolls back on short read) works unchanged for the console branch once the pipe exists — verified it is mode-agnostic.
CLOEXEC on the pipe (start.c:1035-1036) keeps the write end out of the exec'd target, so EOF semantics stay correct.
No existing test pins the current broker-anchored pid for console calls (the ledger's live probe showed the bug ships silently through 135/135).
plays.strongest_objection: This makes the broker unkillable through hold's own verbs: since the record no longer names the broker, a wedged broker (e.g. the post-EOF blocking waitpid finding at broker.c:424) can only be cleaned up with raw kill, and the socket unlink now depends entirely on the broker's goodwill path. Answer honestly: that is already true for the log-capture and waiter processes in non-console mode, and a small optional SIGTERM forwarder in broker_serve covers direct broker kills; but the objection is real and the wedged-waitpid finding should be scheduled next.
plays.unique_insight: The console path does not need new machinery — start.c already has a complete, rollback-safe 'who is the real callee' protocol (target_pid_pipe, gated at start.c:1017 by '!console_mode && !restart_enabled') that the console branch simply opted out of. Flipping the gate to '!restart_enabled' and having the broker (which already knows its forked target at broker.c:189) write one pid through it converts the critical finding into ~30 lines plus a test: the PBX rule that the attendant is never the addressed party was already implemented for plain calls.
